message.content: read agentrail --help to see how to set up a saga, read docs/research.txt for context, read docs/debugger.txt for what this project is trying to build for the related project ../pv24a (p-code COR24 VM) and look at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-* projects for how other UIs are built.  Questions?

content.content:
Workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track

Usage: agentrail [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  init      Initialize a new saga
  setup     Bootstrap a project: create saga, CLAUDE.md, and register domain
  add       Add a step to the saga (for maintenance mode or ad-hoc tasks)
  status    Show current saga state
  next      Output current step prompt and context for a fresh agent session
  begin     Mark the current step as in-progress
  complete  Complete current step, optionally define next step
  plan      View or update the saga plan
  history   Show all step summaries
  distill   Distill trajectories into a skill document
  run-loop  Auto-execute deterministic steps, pause at agent steps
  abort     Mark current step as blocked
  help      Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
      --saga <SAGA>  Path to the project directory (default: current directory) [default: .]
  -h, --help         Print help
  -V, --version      Print version

content.content:
1→
     2→
     3→
     4→
     5→I want to create a Yew/Rust/WASM web app debugger for my custom p-code VM, written in COR24 assembler.  I have similar debuggers for cor24 .s files and for Forth (implemented in .s and .fth) and for Macro Lisp (implemented in C (which was impemented in Rust) and .l24 lisp). So, how do I take the p-code VM and debug it.  Specifically I'm looking for the kind of displays, of what data, which stack(s), which registers, what memory, how to single step p-code.  Start out simple (no watches, no breakpoints).  I've also implemented TSO Pipeline debuggers (w/watches and breakpoints).    Need ideas for what to build, how it will look, use-cases, and how to make it extensible so I could add watches and break-points.  For now being able to see how memory is used, possibly dis-assemble.  What do I not show?  What makes sense?
     6→
     7→You want a Yew/Rust/WASM debugger UI for a custom p-code VM whose implementation lives in COR24 assembler, and you want guidance on:
     8→
     9→what to show
    10→
    11→how it should look
    12→
    13→what data matters
    14→
    15→how to single-step p-code rather than only COR24
    16→
    17→what not to show initially
    18→
    19→how to keep the design extensible for later watches and breakpoints
    20→
    21→Given your existing debugger work for COR24 assembly, Forth, Macro Lisp, and even TSO pipeline visualizations, the right move is not to build “just another machine debugger.” Build a two-level debugger with a strong bias toward the p-code semantic level, while still allowing drill-down into the COR24 implementation when needed.
    22→
    23→1. High-level architecture
    24→Core idea
    25→Your debugger should treat the system as having three layers:
    26→
    27→UI / debugger model layer in Rust/Yew/WASM
    28→
    29→VM semantic layer for p-code execution state
    30→
    31→host implementation layer for COR24 machine state
    32→
    33→That means the debugger is primarily about:
    34→
    35→current p-code instruction
    36→
    37→p-code PC / frame / stack state
    38→
    39→VM-managed memory regions
    40→
    41→mapping from p-code step -> underlying COR24 routine(s)
    42→
    43→The key design principle is:
    44→
    45→Default to showing the p-code machine, not the COR24 machine.
    46→The COR24 state is subordinate and optionally inspectable.
    47→
    48→If you do not enforce that, the debugger turns into an assembler debugger with p-code labels painted on top, which is usually the wrong abstraction for debugging compiler/runtime/VM behavior.
    49→
    50→2. What problem the debugger is solving
    51→You are debugging at least four different classes of bugs:
    52→
    53→A. VM semantic bugs
    54→Examples:
    55→
    56→wrong opcode semantics
    57→
    58→wrong stack effect
    59→
    60→incorrect frame layout
    61→
    62→incorrect local-variable addressing
    63→
    64→incorrect call/return behavior
    65→
    66→bad heap or static data addressing
    67→
    68→wrong p-code dispatch
    69→
    70→B. p-code program bugs
    71→Examples:
    72→
    73→generated p-code is wrong
    74→
    75→compiler emits invalid operand order
    76→
    77→procedure prologue/epilogue mismatch
    78→
    79→incorrect jump targets
    80→
    81→C. VM implementation bugs in COR24
    82→Examples:
    83→
    84→dispatch loop corrupts registers
    85→
    86→VM metadata in memory is wrong
    87→
    88→stack pointer update off by one word
    89→
    90→byte/word addressing bugs
    91→
    92→D. visualization / understanding needs
    93→Examples:
    94→
    95→“where is memory going?”
    96→
    97→“what frame am I in?”
    98→
    99→“why is this return address wrong?”
   100→
   101→“why did this operand get consumed?”
   102→
   103→Because these are mixed, the debugger should let the user move between:
   104→
   105→semantic view
   106→
   107→runtime layout view
   108→
   109→low-level implementation view
   110→
   111→But the semantic view should always be first-class.
   112→
   113→3. The minimum viable debugger
   114→Start with this and no more:
   115→
   116→Main panels
   117→1. Control bar
   118→Buttons:
   119→
   120→Reset
   121→
   122→Run
   123→
   124→Pause
   125→
   126→Step p-code
   127→
   128→Step micro / host
   129→
   130→Step over call
   131→
   132→Step out
   133→
   134→Rewind one step only if you later support trace history
   135→
   136→At minimum, you need only:
   137→
   138→Reset
   139→
   140→Run
   141→
   142→Pause
   143→
   144→Step p-code
   145→
   146→Step host
   147→
   148→2. Current instruction panel
   149→Show:
   150→
   151→current p-code PC
   152→
   153→current decoded p-code instruction
   154→
   155→operands
   156→
   157→stack effect hint
   158→
   159→resolved symbolic target if applicable
   160→
   161→Example:
   162→
   163→PC: 0042
   164→Opcode: CALL 17
   165→Meaning: call procedure "fib"
   166→Stack effect: ( arg0 -- ret0 )
   167→Target paddr: 0100
   168→This should be the main focal point.
   169→
   170→3. P-code stack panel
   171→Show the VM data stack in semantic order, top clearly marked.
   172→
   173→If your p-code machine uses multiple stacks, show them separately:
   174→
   175→operand / evaluation stack
   176→
   177→call stack
   178→
   179→return stack
   180→
   181→control stack
   182→
   183→display / lexical chain stack if applicable
   184→
   185→Do not collapse these conceptually if the VM distinguishes them.
   186→
   187→4. Call frame panel
   188→Show current frame:
   189→
   190→frame pointer / base pointer
   191→
   192→current procedure name or index
   193→
   194→locals
   195→
   196→parameters
   197→
   198→return address
   199→
   200→dynamic link
   201→
   202→static link, if you use one
   203→
   204→This is one of the most useful views and should probably be richer than raw memory.
   205→
   206→5. Memory map panel
   207→Show high-level regions, not only raw hex:
   208→
   209→p-code instruction area
   210→
   211→VM globals
   212→
   213→VM stack(s)
   214→
   215→frame stack
   216→
   217→heap
   218→
   219→literal pool / constants
   220→
   221→host scratch area
   222→
   223→dispatch tables
   224→
   225→A segmented bar plus detailed table works well.
   226→
   227→6. Optional low-level COR24 panel
   228→Collapsed by default.
   229→
   230→Show:
   231→
   232→COR24 PC
   233→
   234→relevant registers
   235→
   236→SP / FP
   237→
   238→selected memory window
   239→
   240→current assembler instruction
   241→
   242→This is for VM implementation debugging, not everyday use.
   243→
   244→4. What state to represent explicitly
   245→Your debugger state model should separate semantic VM state from host machine state.
   246→
   247→VM state
   248→This should be a Rust struct something like:
   249→
   250→struct VmDebugState {
   251→    running: bool,
   252→    halted: bool,
   253→
   254→    pcode_pc: u32,
   255→    current_instr: DecodedPcodeInstr,
   256→
   257→    data_stack: Vec<Value>,
   258→    call_stack: Vec<CallFrame>,
   259→    return_stack: Vec<ReturnEntry>,
   260→
   261→    globals: Vec<MemoryCell>,
   262→    heap_summary: HeapSummary,
   263→    memory_regions: Vec<MemoryRegion>,
   264→
   265→    vm_registers: VmRegisters,
   266→    dispatch_state: DispatchState,
   267→
   268→    source_map: Option<PcodeSourceMap>,
   269→}
   270→Host state
   271→struct HostDebugState {
   272→    cor24_pc: u32,
   273→    cor24_sp: u32,
   274→    cor24_fp: u32,
   275→    regs: [u32; N],
   276→    flags: HostFlags,
   277→
   278→    current_host_instr: Option<DecodedCor24Instr>,
   279→    host_memory_windows: Vec<MemoryWindow>,
   280→}
   281→Combined state
   282→struct DebuggerState {
   283→    vm: VmDebugState,
   284→    host: HostDebugState,
   285→    stepping_mode: SteppingMode,
   286→    trace: Vec<TraceEvent>,
   287→}
   288→This separation matters because it lets you:
   289→
   290→step at different abstraction levels
   291→
   292→hide irrelevant host details
   293→
   294→later add watches and breakpoints at either level
   295→
   296→5. Registers: which ones matter?
   297→You asked specifically which registers to show.
   298→
   299→Show these at the p-code level
   300→Even if they are not physical registers, invent/debug-expose them:
   301→
   302→p-code PC
   303→
   304→current frame pointer
   305→
   306→stack top index
   307→
   308→heap pointer
   309→
   310→current procedure id
   311→
   312→dispatch opcode
   313→
   314→status / halted / fault
   315→
   316→optionally last fault reason
   317→
   318→These are much more meaningful than raw COR24 registers.
   319→
   320→Show these at the host COR24 level
   321→Only show what matters to VM execution:
   322→
   323→PC
   324→
   325→SP
   326→
   327→FP or equivalent
   328→
   329→the few registers the interpreter uses as VM state carriers
   330→
   331→flags if branch/debugging needs them
   332→
   333→Do not show every machine register prominently unless the VM actually depends on them in a meaningful/debuggable way.
   334→
   335→The rule is:
   336→
   337→If a machine register is just transient interpreter plumbing, it should not dominate the UI.
   338→
   339→6. Which stacks to show?
   340→This is one of the most important design choices.
   341→
   342→If your VM has only one stack
   343→Show:
   344→
   345→top few entries
   346→
   347→full scrollable stack
   348→
   349→type or tag if available
   350→
   351→logical index from frame base
   352→
   353→If your VM has multiple stacks
   354→Show each separately, because mixing them causes confusion.
   355→
   356→Typical candidates:
   357→
   358→Data / operand stack
   359→For expression evaluation.
   360→
   361→Call stack
   362→Procedure activations.
   363→
   364→Return stack
   365→Return addresses or continuation records.
   366→
   367→Block / control stack
   368→If needed for loops, exception-like constructs, or nested control flow.
   369→
   370→Display / lexical stack
   371→If your VM supports Pascal-like nested scopes.
   372→
   373→Best practice
   374→Have a Stack Overview panel showing all stacks with:
   375→
   376→name
   377→
   378→depth
   379→
   380→top item
   381→
   382→bounds / region
   383→
   384→Then clicking one opens detailed entries.
   385→
   386→This prevents the UI from becoming too tall while still exposing structure.
   387→
   388→7. Memory: what should the user see?
   389→You should not begin with a single giant hex dump. That is useful only after you already know the bug.
   390→
   391→Instead show memory in layers.
   392→
   393→A. Memory region map
   394→This is the first thing to show.
   395→
   396→Example regions:
   397→
   398→p-code text
   399→
   400→constants
   401→
   402→globals
   403→
   404→activation record stack
   405→
   406→operand stack
   407→
   408→heap
   409→
   410→VM metadata / tables
   411→
   412→host interpreter text/data if you want advanced mode
   413→
   414→Each region should show:
   415→
   416→start
   417→
   418→end
   419→
   420→size
   421→
   422→current usage
   423→
   424→ownership / meaning
   425→
   426→This is great for understanding how memory is used.
   427→
   428→B. Region detail view
   429→When the user selects a region, show a smarter renderer.
   430→
   431→For example:
   432→
   433→p-code text
   434→Show disassembly, not hex.
   435→
   436→stack region
   437→Show stack entries and frame boundaries.
   438→
   439→globals
   440→Show symbolic names if known.
   441→
   442→heap
   443→Show blocks, tags, sizes, links, allocation status.
   444→
   445→C. Raw memory window
   446→Useful, but secondary.
   447→
   448→Hex + ASCII + semantic annotations.
   449→Only one or two windows at first.
   450→
   451→8. Disassembly: yes, but at the right level
   452→You said “possibly dis-assemble.”
   453→Yes, absolutely, but the primary disassembly should be p-code disassembly.
   454→
   455→Show p-code disassembly
   456→A scrollable listing:
   457→
   458→address
   459→
   460→opcode
   461→
   462→operands
   463→
   464→optional labels
   465→
   466→optional symbolic comments
   467→
   468→highlight current PC
   469→
   470→maybe faded marks for recent instructions
   471→
   472→Example:
   473→
   474→0038  LDC   1
   475→0039  LDC   2
   476→0040  ADD
   477→0041  STO   local[0]
   478→0042  CALL  fib
   479→0043  JMP   0050
   480→Secondary: host disassembly
   481→This is for the COR24 interpreter implementation.
   482→
   483→Useful for:
   484→
   485→dispatch bugs
   486→
   487→entry/exit sequence bugs
   488→
   489→stack corruption inside interpreter
   490→
   491→But it should be hidden or in an “implementation” tab.
   492→
   493→9. How to single-step p-code
   494→This is the hardest architectural point.
   495→
   496→You need to decide what a single p-code step means relative to the underlying COR24 interpreter.
   497→
   498→Correct model
   499→A p-code step should mean:
   500→
   501→Execute exactly one semantic p-code instruction, even if it takes many COR24 instructions.
   502→
   503→That means your VM/interpreter needs a notion of:
   504→
   505→instruction boundary
   506→
   507→before-state
   508→
   509→after-state
   510→
   511→Best implementation options
   512→Option 1: explicit interpreter hook at dispatch boundary
   513→At the start and/or end of each p-code dispatch:
   514→
   515→capture VM state snapshot
   516→
   517→notify debugger
   518→
   519→pause after one p-code instruction if stepping mode says so
   520→
   521→This is the cleanest.
   522→
   523→Pseudo-logic:
   524→
   525→loop:
   526→  fetch opcode at pcode_pc
   527→  debugger_event(BeforePcodeStep)
   528→  execute opcode
   529→  debugger_event(AfterPcodeStep)
   530→  if step_mode == PcodeSingleStep: pause
   531→  goto loop
   532→Option 2: instrument each opcode handler
   533→Each handler reports:
   534→
   535→entered opcode X
   536→
   537→completed opcode X
   538→
   539→faulted in opcode X
   540→
   541→This is useful if some handlers internally branch or call helpers.
   542→
   543→Option 3: infer boundary from host PC ranges
   544→Least desirable.
   545→
   546→You identify that host addresses A..B implement opcode ADD, etc.
   547→This is fragile and should only be supplementary.
   548→
   549→Recommendation
   550→Use explicit semantic instrumentation in the VM, even if written in COR24 assembler.
   551→
   552→You do not want the browser debugger reconstructing p-code semantics from raw host traces.
   553→
   554→10. Step modes that make sense
   555→You asked about single stepping. Start with only a few modes.
   556→
   557→1. Step p-code
   558→Executes one semantic VM instruction.
   559→
   560→This should be the default.
   561→
   562→2. Step host
   563→Executes one COR24 instruction.
   564→
   565→Advanced mode for VM debugging.
   566→
   567→3. Step over call
   568→At p-code level:
   569→
   570→if current opcode is CALL, run until return to caller
   571→
   572→otherwise same as Step p-code
   573→
   574→Very useful early.
   575→
   576→4. Step out
   577→Run until current frame exits.
   578→
   579→Useful once call frames are visible.
   580→
   581→Do not start with step into/over/out plus breakpoints plus watches plus reverse debugging. Start with p-code step and host step.
   582→
   583→11. What the UI should look like
   584→A simple layout that will work well in Yew:
   585→
   586→Top row
   587→control bar
   588→
   589→run state
   590→
   591→current mode
   592→
   593→current procedure / p-code PC
   594→
   595→Left column
   596→p-code disassembly
   597→
   598→current instruction details
   599→
   600→Center column
   601→current frame
   602→
   603→data stack
   604→
   605→call stack
   606→
   607→Right column
   608→memory region map
   609→
   610→selected memory region detail
   611→
   612→collapsed host/COR24 panel
   613→
   614→A rough mental model:
   615→
   616→+---------------------------------------------------------------+
   617→| Controls: Reset Run Pause Step-P Step-H StepOver StepOut      |
   618→+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
   619→| P-code Disassembly   | Frame / Locals       | Memory Map       |
   620→| current line hilite  | Params / Return addr | Regions          |
   621→| nearby instructions  | Dynamic/static links | Usage bars       |
   622→+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
   623→| Current Instr Detail | Data Stack           | Region Detail    |
   624→| opcode operands      | top -> bottom        | stack/heap/glob  |
   625→| stack effect         | typed/tagged         |                  |
   626→+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
   627→| Host / COR24 (collapsed by default)                            |
   628→+---------------------------------------------------------------+
   629→This layout favors the semantic view.
   630→
   631→12. What not to show initially
   632→This is as important as what to show.
   633→
   634→Do not show everything all at once
   635→Avoid starting with:
   636→
   637→every COR24 register always visible
   638→
   639→huge hex dumps everywhere
   640→
   641→watch panes with no watch model yet
   642→
   643→source-level data if you do not have reliable symbol maps
   644→
   645→pipeline/TSO-style timing clutter unless the VM is truly concurrent
   646→
   647→microarchitectural internals irrelevant to p-code semantics
   648→
   649→Specifically avoid these mistakes
   650→Mistake 1: host state dominates screen space
   651→Then users debug the interpreter instead of the VM program.
   652→
   653→Mistake 2: raw memory before semantic views
   654→Then users hunt bytes instead of understanding frames and stacks.
   655→
   656→Mistake 3: too many panes
   657→Yew/WASM UI can become visually busy fast.
   658→
   659→Mistake 4: pretending types exist when they do not
   660→If values are untyped words, show that honestly.
   661→If tags exist, show tags.
   662→Do not invent too much false structure.
   663→
   664→13. What makes sense to annotate
   665→Your debugger gets much better if it annotates data, even lightly.
   666→
   667→Good annotations
   668→current procedure name
   669→
   670→local slot names
   671→
   672→parameter names
   673→
   674→frame boundaries
   675→
   676→jump targets with labels
   677→
   678→constants decoded symbolically
   679→
   680→stack effect hints
   681→
   682→which memory word is “return address”
   683→
   684→which words are “saved frame pointer”, “dynamic link”, etc.
   685→
   686→Minimal metadata format
   687→You likely want a sidecar debug-symbol structure:
   688→
   689→struct PcodeDebugSymbols {
   690→    procedures: Vec<ProcedureSymbol>,
   691→    labels: Vec<LabelSymbol>,
   692→    globals: Vec<GlobalSymbol>,
   693→    local_maps: HashMap<ProcId, Vec<LocalSymbol>>,
   694→    comments: HashMap<u32, String>,
   695→}
   696→This can come from:
   697→
   698→the p-code assembler/compiler
   699→
   700→manually authored metadata
   701→
   702→generated tables
   703→
   704→This is optional at first but highly valuable.
   705→
   706→14. Use-cases the debugger should support
   707→Design around concrete scenarios.
   708→
   709→Use-case 1: basic instruction semantics
   710→“I step ADD and want to see two operands consumed and one result produced.”
   711→
   712→Needed views:
   713→
   714→current opcode
   715→
   716→operand stack before/after
   717→
   718→p-code PC change
   719→
   720→Use-case 2: call/return correctness
   721→“I call procedure P and want to confirm frame layout and return address.”
   722→
   723→Needed views:
   724→
   725→current frame
   726→
   727→call stack
   728→
   729→locals/params
   730→
   731→step over / step out
   732→
   733→Use-case 3: jump target correctness
   734→“I think a conditional branch goes to the wrong offset.”
   735→
   736→Needed views:
   737→
   738→p-code disassembly
   739→
   740→current instruction detail
   741→
   742→stack top / condition value
   743→
   744→target label
   745→
   746→Use-case 4: memory corruption
   747→“My locals get overwritten after nested calls.”
   748→
   749→Needed views:
   750→
   751→frame panel
   752→
   753→memory region map
   754→
   755→stack region detail
   756→
   757→maybe host step for interpreter bug
   758→
   759→Use-case 5: dispatch implementation bug
   760→“The p-code ADD handler works unless preceded by CALL.”
   761→
   762→Needed views:
   763→
   764→p-code step
   765→
   766→then drill down to host/COR24 step
   767→
   768→host registers / host PC / selected memory window
   769→
   770→This is why dual-level stepping matters.
   771→
   772→15. Extensibility for watches and breakpoints
   773→You asked how to make it extensible.
   774→
   775→Use an event-driven debugger core
   776→Your execution engine should emit events like:
   777→
   778→enum DebugEvent {
   779→    VmReset,
   780→    BeforePcodeStep { pc: u32, instr: DecodedPcodeInstr },
   781→    AfterPcodeStep { pc: u32, instr: DecodedPcodeInstr },
   782→    BeforeHostStep { pc: u32 },
   783→    AfterHostStep { pc: u32 },
   784→    MemoryWrite { addr: u32, old: u32, new: u32 },
   785→    StackPush { stack: StackKind, value: Value },
   786→    StackPop { stack: StackKind, value: Value },
   787→    Call { target: u32, new_frame: CallFrame },
   788→    Return { from: u32, to: u32 },
   789→    Fault { reason: VmFault },
   790→}
   791→This makes later features natural.
   792→
   793→Breakpoints later become predicates on events/state
   794→Examples:
   795→
   796→p-code PC == 42
   797→
   798→procedure == fib
   799→
   800→memory write to address range
   801→
   802→stack depth exceeds N
   803→
   804→call to procedure X
   805→
   806→Watches later become derived views
   807→Examples:
   808→
   809→show local[3]
   810→
   811→show global G
   812→
   813→show memory[0x1234]
   814→
   815→show stack top
   816→
   817→show heap usage
   818→
   819→If your debugger core already maintains explicit state snapshots and events, these are straightforward.
   820→
   821→16. Suggested data model for the UI
   822→A clean Yew architecture would separate:
   823→
   824→A. immutable-ish snapshot state
   825→What the VM looks like now.
   826→
   827→B. derived selectors
   828→Functions that compute:
   829→
   830→visible stack slice
   831→
   832→current procedure
   833→
   834→current frame locals
   835→
   836→current region occupancy
   837→
   838→surrounding disassembly window
   839→
   840→C. UI state
   841→What the user has expanded/selected:
   842→
   843→selected stack
   844→
   845→selected memory region
   846→
   847→selected disassembly line
   848→
   849→show host panel or not
   850→
   851→current stepping mode
   852→
   853→That keeps the rendering logic sane.
   854→
   855→17. A good phased plan
   856→Phase 1: semantic debugger only
   857→Build:
   858→
   859→controls
   860→
   861→current p-code instruction
   862→
   863→p-code disassembly
   864→
   865→operand stack
   866→
   867→call stack / frame view
   868→
   869→memory region map
   870→
   871→No raw host state yet except maybe a tiny status line.
   872→
   873→Phase 2: region detail
   874→Add:
   875→
   876→frame-aware stack rendering
   877→
   878→globals rendering
   879→
   880→heap summary
   881→
   882→selected memory region viewer
   883→
   884→Phase 3: host drill-down
   885→Add:
   886→
   887→COR24 PC / registers
   888→
   889→host disassembly around PC
   890→
   891→step host
   892→
   893→Phase 4: richer step control
   894→Add:
   895→
   896→step over
   897→
   898→step out
   899→
   900→run until next call/return/fault
   901→
   902→Phase 5: event history
   903→Add:
   904→
   905→last N events
   906→
   907→last memory writes
   908→
   909→last stack operations
   910→
   911→This is often more useful than watches early on.
   912→
   913→Phase 6: breakpoints and watches
   914→Only after the above feels solid.
   915→
   916→18. One especially useful feature: “why did this change?”
   917→Even without watches, you can add a very strong aid:
   918→
   919→Delta highlighting between p-code steps
   920→After each step, highlight:
   921→
   922→which stack entries changed
   923→
   924→which memory words changed
   925→
   926→which frame fields changed
   927→
   928→how p-code PC changed
   929→
   930→This can be presented as:
   931→
   932→Step 41 -> 42
   933→- data stack: pop 2, pop 3, push 5
   934→- frame unchanged
   935→- globals unchanged
   936→- pcode_pc: 0040 -> 0041
   937→This is incredibly useful and much cheaper than a full reverse debugger.
   938→
   939→19. What likely matters less at first
   940→Unless your VM really depends on them, I would postpone:
   941→
   942→cycle counts
   943→
   944→host instruction counts per p-code op
   945→
   946→pipeline timing
   947→
   948→cache-like effects
   949→
   950→raw binary sections of the host interpreter
   951→
   952→complex heap graph visualizations
   953→
   954→expression evaluators for watch windows
   955→
   956→source-level AST rendering
   957→
   958→You can add these later, but they are not the shortest path to a useful p-code debugger.
   959→
   960→20. My recommendation: the initial concrete screen
   961→If I were building v1, I would implement exactly this:
   962→
   963→Top
   964→Reset | Run | Pause | Step P | Step H | Over | Out
   965→
   966→status: Running / Paused / Faulted
   967→
   968→p-code PC, current proc, current frame depth
   969→
   970→Left pane
   971→P-code Disassembly
   972→
   973→20-50 instructions around PC
   974→
   975→current line highlighted
   976→
   977→labels shown
   978→
   979→target lines lightly marked
   980→
   981→Middle pane top
   982→Current Instruction
   983→
   984→opcode
   985→
   986→operands
   987→
   988→short semantic description
   989→
   990→stack effect
   991→
   992→Middle pane middle
   993→Operand Stack
   994→
   995→top first
   996→
   997→changed values highlighted
   998→
   999→Middle pane bottom
  1000→Call Frame
  1001→
  1002→proc name
  1003→
  1004→params
  1005→
  1006→locals
  1007→
  1008→return addr
  1009→
  1010→links
  1011→
  1012→Right pane top
  1013→Memory Regions
  1014→
  1015→segmented bar
  1016→
  1017→usage stats
  1018→
  1019→Right pane bottom
  1020→Region Detail
  1021→
  1022→selected region rendered semantically
  1023→
  1024→Bottom collapsed drawer
  1025→Host / COR24
  1026→
  1027→PC
  1028→
  1029→SP/FP
  1030→
  1031→a few regs
  1032→
  1033→nearby host disassembly
  1034→
  1035→That is enough to be genuinely useful.
  1036→
  1037→21. Short answer to “what do I not show?”
  1038→Do not lead with:
  1039→
  1040→full machine-state complexity
  1041→
  1042→giant raw hex memory dumps
  1043→
  1044→microarchitectural detail
  1045→
  1046→all registers
  1047→
  1048→watch/breakpoint UI shells before the stepping model is correct
  1049→
  1050→Do show:
  1051→
  1052→p-code PC
  1053→
  1054→decoded p-code instruction
  1055→
  1056→semantic stacks
  1057→
  1058→call frame
  1059→
  1060→memory regions
  1061→
  1062→deltas after each step
  1063→
  1064→optional low-level drill-down
  1065→
  1066→22. Design mantra
  1067→A good debugger for a VM answers these questions quickly:
  1068→
  1069→What p-code instruction am I on?
  1070→
  1071→What abstract machine state does it see?
  1072→
  1073→What changed when I stepped it?
  1074→
  1075→Where am I in procedure/frame/memory terms?
  1076→
  1077→Can I drill down to the COR24 implementation when needed?
  1078→
  1079→If your UI answers those five well, it will already be strong.
  1080→
  1081→23. Best next artifact to create
  1082→The next thing worth writing is a short design doc with sections like:
  1083→
  1084→goals
  1085→
  1086→non-goals
  1087→
  1088→abstraction layers
  1089→
  1090→debugger state model
  1091→
  1092→event model
  1093→
  1094→initial screen layout
  1095→
  1096→step semantics
  1097→
  1098→extensibility points
  1099→
  1100→After that, define the Rust structs for:
  1101→
  1102→VmDebugState
  1103→
  1104→HostDebugState
  1105→
  1106→DebugEvent
  1107→
  1108→MemoryRegion
  1109→
  1110→CallFrame
  1111→
  1112→DecodedPcodeInstr
  1113→
  1114→Then build the UI against mocked JSON snapshots before wiring it to the live VM.
  1115→
  1116→I can draft that design doc and the initial Rust/Yew type definitions next.

file.content:
I want to create a Yew/Rust/WASM web app debugger for my custom p-code VM, written in COR24 assembler.  I have similar debuggers for cor24 .s files and for Forth (implemented in .s and .fth) and for Macro Lisp (implemented in C (which was impemented in Rust) and .l24 lisp). So, how do I take the p-code VM and debug it.  Specifically I'm looking for the kind of displays, of what data, which stack(s), which registers, what memory, how to single step p-code.  Start out simple (no watches, no breakpoints).  I've also implemented TSO Pipeline debuggers (w/watches and breakpoints).    Need ideas for what to build, how it will look, use-cases, and how to make it extensible so I could add watches and break-points.  For now being able to see how memory is used, possibly dis-assemble.  What do I not show?  What makes sense?

You want a Yew/Rust/WASM debugger UI for a custom p-code VM whose implementation lives in COR24 assembler, and you want guidance on:

what to show

how it should look

what data matters

how to single-step p-code rather than only COR24

what not to show initially

how to keep the design extensible for later watches and breakpoints

Given your existing debugger work for COR24 assembly, Forth, Macro Lisp, and even TSO pipeline visualizations, the right move is not to build “just another machine debugger.” Build a two-level debugger with a strong bias toward the p-code semantic level, while still allowing drill-down into the COR24 implementation when needed.

1. High-level architecture
Core idea
Your debugger should treat the system as having three layers:

UI / debugger model layer in Rust/Yew/WASM

VM semantic layer for p-code execution state

host implementation layer for COR24 machine state

That means the debugger is primarily about:

current p-code instruction

p-code PC / frame / stack state

VM-managed memory regions

mapping from p-code step -> underlying COR24 routine(s)

The key design principle is:

Default to showing the p-code machine, not the COR24 machine.
The COR24 state is subordinate and optionally inspectable.

If you do not enforce that, the debugger turns into an assembler debugger with p-code labels painted on top, which is usually the wrong abstraction for debugging compiler/runtime/VM behavior.

2. What problem the debugger is solving
You are debugging at least four different classes of bugs:

A. VM semantic bugs
Examples:

wrong opcode semantics

wrong stack effect

incorrect frame layout

incorrect local-variable addressing

incorrect call/return behavior

bad heap or static data addressing

wrong p-code dispatch

B. p-code program bugs
Examples:

generated p-code is wrong

compiler emits invalid operand order

procedure prologue/epilogue mismatch

incorrect jump targets

C. VM implementation bugs in COR24
Examples:

dispatch loop corrupts registers

VM metadata in memory is wrong

stack pointer update off by one word

byte/word addressing bugs

D. visualization / understanding needs
Examples:

“where is memory going?”

“what frame am I in?”

“why is this return address wrong?”

“why did this operand get consumed?”

Because these are mixed, the debugger should let the user move between:

semantic view

runtime layout view

low-level implementation view

But the semantic view should always be first-class.

3. The minimum viable debugger
Start with this and no more:

Main panels
1. Control bar
Buttons:

Reset

Run

Pause

Step p-code

Step micro / host

Step over call

Step out

Rewind one step only if you later support trace history

At minimum, you need only:

Reset

Run

Pause

Step p-code

Step host

2. Current instruction panel
Show:

current p-code PC

current decoded p-code instruction

operands

stack effect hint

resolved symbolic target if applicable

Example:

PC: 0042
Opcode: CALL 17
Meaning: call procedure "fib"
Stack effect: ( arg0 -- ret0 )
Target paddr: 0100
This should be the main focal point.

3. P-code stack panel
Show the VM data stack in semantic order, top clearly marked.

If your p-code machine uses multiple stacks, show them separately:

operand / evaluation stack

call stack

return stack

control stack

display / lexical chain stack if applicable

Do not collapse these conceptually if the VM distinguishes them.

4. Call frame panel
Show current frame:

frame pointer / base pointer

current procedure name or index

locals

parameters

return address

dynamic link

static link, if you use one

This is one of the most useful views and should probably be richer than raw memory.

5. Memory map panel
Show high-level regions, not only raw hex:

p-code instruction area

VM globals

VM stack(s)

frame stack

heap

literal pool / constants

host scratch area

dispatch tables

A segmented bar plus detailed table works well.

6. Optional low-level COR24 panel
Collapsed by default.

Show:

COR24 PC

relevant registers

SP / FP

selected memory window

current assembler instruction

This is for VM implementation debugging, not everyday use.

4. What state to represent explicitly
Your debugger state model should separate semantic VM state from host machine state.

VM state
This should be a Rust struct something like:

struct VmDebugState {
    running: bool,
    halted: bool,

    pcode_pc: u32,
    current_instr: DecodedPcodeInstr,

    data_stack: Vec<Value>,
    call_stack: Vec<CallFrame>,
    return_stack: Vec<ReturnEntry>,

    globals: Vec<MemoryCell>,
    heap_summary: HeapSummary,
    memory_regions: Vec<MemoryRegion>,

    vm_registers: VmRegisters,
    dispatch_state: DispatchState,

    source_map: Option<PcodeSourceMap>,
}
Host state
struct HostDebugState {
    cor24_pc: u32,
    cor24_sp: u32,
    cor24_fp: u32,
    regs: [u32; N],
    flags: HostFlags,

    current_host_instr: Option<DecodedCor24Instr>,
    host_memory_windows: Vec<MemoryWindow>,
}
Combined state
struct DebuggerState {
    vm: VmDebugState,
    host: HostDebugState,
    stepping_mode: SteppingMode,
    trace: Vec<TraceEvent>,
}
This separation matters because it lets you:

step at different abstraction levels

hide irrelevant host details

later add watches and breakpoints at either level

5. Registers: which ones matter?
You asked specifically which registers to show.

Show these at the p-code level
Even if they are not physical registers, invent/debug-expose them:

p-code PC

current frame pointer

stack top index

heap pointer

current procedure id

dispatch opcode

status / halted / fault

optionally last fault reason

These are much more meaningful than raw COR24 registers.

Show these at the host COR24 level
Only show what matters to VM execution:

PC

SP

FP or equivalent

the few registers the interpreter uses as VM state carriers

flags if branch/debugging needs them

Do not show every machine register prominently unless the VM actually depends on them in a meaningful/debuggable way.

The rule is:

If a machine register is just transient interpreter plumbing, it should not dominate the UI.

6. Which stacks to show?
This is one of the most important design choices.

If your VM has only one stack
Show:

top few entries

full scrollable stack

type or tag if available

logical index from frame base

If your VM has multiple stacks
Show each separately, because mixing them causes confusion.

Typical candidates:

Data / operand stack
For expression evaluation.

Call stack
Procedure activations.

Return stack
Return addresses or continuation records.

Block / control stack
If needed for loops, exception-like constructs, or nested control flow.

Display / lexical stack
If your VM supports Pascal-like nested scopes.

Best practice
Have a Stack Overview panel showing all stacks with:

name

depth

top item

bounds / region

Then clicking one opens detailed entries.

This prevents the UI from becoming too tall while still exposing structure.

7. Memory: what should the user see?
You should not begin with a single giant hex dump. That is useful only after you already know the bug.

Instead show memory in layers.

A. Memory region map
This is the first thing to show.

Example regions:

p-code text

constants

globals

activation record stack

operand stack

heap

VM metadata / tables

host interpreter text/data if you want advanced mode

Each region should show:

start

end

size

current usage

ownership / meaning

This is great for understanding how memory is used.

B. Region detail view
When the user selects a region, show a smarter renderer.

For example:

p-code text
Show disassembly, not hex.

stack region
Show stack entries and frame boundaries.

globals
Show symbolic names if known.

heap
Show blocks, tags, sizes, links, allocation status.

C. Raw memory window
Useful, but secondary.

Hex + ASCII + semantic annotations.
Only one or two windows at first.

8. Disassembly: yes, but at the right level
You said “possibly dis-assemble.”
Yes, absolutely, but the primary disassembly should be p-code disassembly.

Show p-code disassembly
A scrollable listing:

address

opcode

operands

optional labels

optional symbolic comments

highlight current PC

maybe faded marks for recent instructions

Example:

0038  LDC   1
0039  LDC   2
0040  ADD
0041  STO   local[0]
0042  CALL  fib
0043  JMP   0050
Secondary: host disassembly
This is for the COR24 interpreter implementation.

Useful for:

dispatch bugs

entry/exit sequence bugs

stack corruption inside interpreter

But it should be hidden or in an “implementation” tab.

9. How to single-step p-code
This is the hardest architectural point.

You need to decide what a single p-code step means relative to the underlying COR24 interpreter.

Correct model
A p-code step should mean:

Execute exactly one semantic p-code instruction, even if it takes many COR24 instructions.

That means your VM/interpreter needs a notion of:

instruction boundary

before-state

after-state

Best implementation options
Option 1: explicit interpreter hook at dispatch boundary
At the start and/or end of each p-code dispatch:

capture VM state snapshot

notify debugger

pause after one p-code instruction if stepping mode says so

This is the cleanest.

Pseudo-logic:

loop:
  fetch opcode at pcode_pc
  debugger_event(BeforePcodeStep)
  execute opcode
  debugger_event(AfterPcodeStep)
  if step_mode == PcodeSingleStep: pause
  goto loop
Option 2: instrument each opcode handler
Each handler reports:

entered opcode X

completed opcode X

faulted in opcode X

This is useful if some handlers internally branch or call helpers.

Option 3: infer boundary from host PC ranges
Least desirable.

You identify that host addresses A..B implement opcode ADD, etc.
This is fragile and should only be supplementary.

Recommendation
Use explicit semantic instrumentation in the VM, even if written in COR24 assembler.

You do not want the browser debugger reconstructing p-code semantics from raw host traces.

10. Step modes that make sense
You asked about single stepping. Start with only a few modes.

1. Step p-code
Executes one semantic VM instruction.

This should be the default.

2. Step host
Executes one COR24 instruction.

Advanced mode for VM debugging.

3. Step over call
At p-code level:

if current opcode is CALL, run until return to caller

otherwise same as Step p-code

Very useful early.

4. Step out
Run until current frame exits.

Useful once call frames are visible.

Do not start with step into/over/out plus breakpoints plus watches plus reverse debugging. Start with p-code step and host step.

11. What the UI should look like
A simple layout that will work well in Yew:

Top row
control bar

run state

current mode

current procedure / p-code PC

Left column
p-code disassembly

current instruction details

Center column
current frame

data stack

call stack

Right column
memory region map

selected memory region detail

collapsed host/COR24 panel

A rough mental model:

+---------------------------------------------------------------+
| Controls: Reset Run Pause Step-P Step-H StepOver StepOut      |
+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
| P-code Disassembly   | Frame / Locals       | Memory Map       |
| current line hilite  | Params / Return addr | Regions          |
| nearby instructions  | Dynamic/static links | Usage bars       |
+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
| Current Instr Detail | Data Stack           | Region Detail    |
| opcode operands      | top -> bottom        | stack/heap/glob  |
| stack effect         | typed/tagged         |                  |
+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
| Host / COR24 (collapsed by default)                            |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
This layout favors the semantic view.

12. What not to show initially
This is as important as what to show.

Do not show everything all at once
Avoid starting with:

every COR24 register always visible

huge hex dumps everywhere

watch panes with no watch model yet

source-level data if you do not have reliable symbol maps

pipeline/TSO-style timing clutter unless the VM is truly concurrent

microarchitectural internals irrelevant to p-code semantics

Specifically avoid these mistakes
Mistake 1: host state dominates screen space
Then users debug the interpreter instead of the VM program.

Mistake 2: raw memory before semantic views
Then users hunt bytes instead of understanding frames and stacks.

Mistake 3: too many panes
Yew/WASM UI can become visually busy fast.

Mistake 4: pretending types exist when they do not
If values are untyped words, show that honestly.
If tags exist, show tags.
Do not invent too much false structure.

13. What makes sense to annotate
Your debugger gets much better if it annotates data, even lightly.

Good annotations
current procedure name

local slot names

parameter names

frame boundaries

jump targets with labels

constants decoded symbolically

stack effect hints

which memory word is “return address”

which words are “saved frame pointer”, “dynamic link”, etc.

Minimal metadata format
You likely want a sidecar debug-symbol structure:

struct PcodeDebugSymbols {
    procedures: Vec<ProcedureSymbol>,
    labels: Vec<LabelSymbol>,
    globals: Vec<GlobalSymbol>,
    local_maps: HashMap<ProcId, Vec<LocalSymbol>>,
    comments: HashMap<u32, String>,
}
This can come from:

the p-code assembler/compiler

manually authored metadata

generated tables

This is optional at first but highly valuable.

14. Use-cases the debugger should support
Design around concrete scenarios.

Use-case 1: basic instruction semantics
“I step ADD and want to see two operands consumed and one result produced.”

Needed views:

current opcode

operand stack before/after

p-code PC change

Use-case 2: call/return correctness
“I call procedure P and want to confirm frame layout and return address.”

Needed views:

current frame

call stack

locals/params

step over / step out

Use-case 3: jump target correctness
“I think a conditional branch goes to the wrong offset.”

Needed views:

p-code disassembly

current instruction detail

stack top / condition value

target label

Use-case 4: memory corruption
“My locals get overwritten after nested calls.”

Needed views:

frame panel

memory region map

stack region detail

maybe host step for interpreter bug

Use-case 5: dispatch implementation bug
“The p-code ADD handler works unless preceded by CALL.”

Needed views:

p-code step

then drill down to host/COR24 step

host registers / host PC / selected memory window

This is why dual-level stepping matters.

15. Extensibility for watches and breakpoints
You asked how to make it extensible.

Use an event-driven debugger core
Your execution engine should emit events like:

enum DebugEvent {
    VmReset,
    BeforePcodeStep { pc: u32, instr: DecodedPcodeInstr },
    AfterPcodeStep { pc: u32, instr: DecodedPcodeInstr },
    BeforeHostStep { pc: u32 },
    AfterHostStep { pc: u32 },
    MemoryWrite { addr: u32, old: u32, new: u32 },
    StackPush { stack: StackKind, value: Value },
    StackPop { stack: StackKind, value: Value },
    Call { target: u32, new_frame: CallFrame },
    Return { from: u32, to: u32 },
    Fault { reason: VmFault },
}
This makes later features natural.

Breakpoints later become predicates on events/state
Examples:

p-code PC == 42

procedure == fib

memory write to address range

stack depth exceeds N

call to procedure X

Watches later become derived views
Examples:

show local[3]

show global G

show memory[0x1234]

show stack top

show heap usage

If your debugger core already maintains explicit state snapshots and events, these are straightforward.

16. Suggested data model for the UI
A clean Yew architecture would separate:

A. immutable-ish snapshot state
What the VM looks like now.

B. derived selectors
Functions that compute:

visible stack slice

current procedure

current frame locals

current region occupancy

surrounding disassembly window

C. UI state
What the user has expanded/selected:

selected stack

selected memory region

selected disassembly line

show host panel or not

current stepping mode

That keeps the rendering logic sane.

17. A good phased plan
Phase 1: semantic debugger only
Build:

controls

current p-code instruction

p-code disassembly

operand stack

call stack / frame view

memory region map

No raw host state yet except maybe a tiny status line.

Phase 2: region detail
Add:

frame-aware stack rendering

globals rendering

heap summary

selected memory region viewer

Phase 3: host drill-down
Add:

COR24 PC / registers

host disassembly around PC

step host

Phase 4: richer step control
Add:

step over

step out

run until next call/return/fault

Phase 5: event history
Add:

last N events

last memory writes

last stack operations

This is often more useful than watches early on.

Phase 6: breakpoints and watches
Only after the above feels solid.

18. One especially useful feature: “why did this change?”
Even without watches, you can add a very strong aid:

Delta highlighting between p-code steps
After each step, highlight:

which stack entries changed

which memory words changed

which frame fields changed

how p-code PC changed

This can be presented as:

Step 41 -> 42
- data stack: pop 2, pop 3, push 5
- frame unchanged
- globals unchanged
- pcode_pc: 0040 -> 0041
This is incredibly useful and much cheaper than a full reverse debugger.

19. What likely matters less at first
Unless your VM really depends on them, I would postpone:

cycle counts

host instruction counts per p-code op

pipeline timing

cache-like effects

raw binary sections of the host interpreter

complex heap graph visualizations

expression evaluators for watch windows

source-level AST rendering

You can add these later, but they are not the shortest path to a useful p-code debugger.

20. My recommendation: the initial concrete screen
If I were building v1, I would implement exactly this:

Top
Reset | Run | Pause | Step P | Step H | Over | Out

status: Running / Paused / Faulted

p-code PC, current proc, current frame depth

Left pane
P-code Disassembly

20-50 instructions around PC

current line highlighted

labels shown

target lines lightly marked

Middle pane top
Current Instruction

opcode

operands

short semantic description

stack effect

Middle pane middle
Operand Stack

top first

changed values highlighted

Middle pane bottom
Call Frame

proc name

params

locals

return addr

links

Right pane top
Memory Regions

segmented bar

usage stats

Right pane bottom
Region Detail

selected region rendered semantically

Bottom collapsed drawer
Host / COR24

PC

SP/FP

a few regs

That is enough to be genuinely useful.

21. Short answer to “what do I not show?”
Do not lead with:

full machine-state complexity

giant raw hex memory dumps

microarchitectural detail

all registers

watch/breakpoint UI shells before the stepping model is correct

Do show:

p-code PC

decoded p-code instruction

semantic stacks

call frame

memory regions

deltas after each step

optional low-level drill-down

22. Design mantra
A good debugger for a VM answers these questions quickly:

What p-code instruction am I on?

What abstract machine state does it see?

What changed when I stepped it?

Where am I in procedure/frame/memory terms?

Can I drill down to the COR24 implementation when needed?

If your UI answers those five well, it will already be strong.

23. Best next artifact to create
The next thing worth writing is a short design doc with sections like:

goals

non-goals

abstraction layers

debugger state model

event model

initial screen layout

step semantics

extensibility points

After that, define the Rust structs for:

VmDebugState

HostDebugState

DebugEvent

MemoryRegion

CallFrame

DecodedPcodeInstr

Then build the UI against mocked JSON snapshots before wiring it to the live VM.

I can draft that design doc and the initial Rust/Yew type definitions next.
