file.content:
How would I implement Pascal from scratch for a custom 24-bit ISA?  I have a working C compiler and Assembler (and emulator and FPGA hardware).  Was Pascal bootstapped in assembler or C and then written in Pascal?  Are there open source implementations with liberal licenses (BSD, MIT) that I can look at?  I do not want to port from a 16-bit or 32-bit system.  I prefer to write-from-scratch, clean-room implementation, possibly using already-written Pascal souce examples for testing my compiler.  I also want a debugger.  How should I start?  


Thought for 36s
Given your constraints, I would not start by trying to build a self-hosting Pascal compiler immediately.

You already have the right base:

working assembler

working C compiler

emulator + FPGA target

desire for a clean-room implementation

debugger requirement from day one

That points to a practical architecture:

Recommended architecture
Build Pascal in three stages:

Pascal front end in C

lexer

parser

AST

symbol tables

type checker

simple error recovery

Portable intermediate form

either a small stack VM / p-code

or a very small typed IR of your own

Back ends

first: interpreter for the IR/p-code on the host

second: native code generator for your 24-bit ISA

later: optional self-hosting compiler in Pascal

That is the lowest-risk path because it lets you validate semantics before you fight register allocation, calling conventions, and codegen bugs.

Historically, how Pascal was bootstrapped
The earliest Pascal compiler was not originally bootstrapped in assembler in the way many people assume. Niklaus Wirth’s first FORTRAN-based attempt was unsuccessful; the successful implementation was done in a C-like language called Scallop and then translated by hand into Pascal for bootstrapping. Many later Pascal compilers became self-hosting. A famous exception is GNU Pascal, which was written in C. Turbo Pascal, much later, was famously written in assembly for speed and size. 

So the historical pattern is roughly:

prototype in another language

get a working compiler

rewrite or re-express the compiler in Pascal

use the previous compiler to compile the new compiler

That is exactly the path I would recommend for your ISA as well.

What to target first: native code or p-code?
For your situation, I would start with a Pascal-S / Pascal-P style subset and a p-code-like intermediate machine.

Why:

Pascal semantics are nontrivial enough already: nested procedures, static scoping, sets, records, packed/unpacked data, file types, pass-by-reference, variant records, etc.

your ISA is 24-bit, which means you will be making many nonstandard representation decisions anyway

a p-code interpreter gives you a correctness oracle before native codegen

Historically, Pascal-P was created specifically as a compiler porting kit: Pascal source compiled to p-code for a virtual stack machine, then interpreted on each target. That was done to make Pascal portable. 

For a custom ISA, that strategy is still excellent.

What subset should you implement first
Do not begin with full ISO Pascal.

Begin with a clean educational subset:

Phase 0: expression language
integers only

constants

variables

assignment

arithmetic

comparisons

if, while

begin ... end

writeln-like builtin for debugging

Phase 1: real Pascal block structure
program

declarations

scalar types: integer, boolean, char

arrays with fixed bounds

records

procedures/functions

lexical nesting

local variables

pass-by-value and var parameters

Phase 2: features that force runtime design
strings or packed char arrays

sets

pointers

heap

files

case statements

standard procedures/functions

Phase 3: optional/full language
ISO-ish cleanup

conformance tests

self-hosting subset

For your first compiler, avoid:

packed types

files

variant records

conformant arrays

procedural parameters

goto

real numbers

Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.

Clean-room strategy
Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.

A good clean-room posture is:

read language reports and public docs

study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers

do not copy implementation code

write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen

use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs

That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.

Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:

Stage A — host-side compiler in C
Run on your current host machine.

Outputs either:

textual p-code / bytecode

or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA

This lets you iterate quickly.

Stage B — target runtime
On your ISA:

tiny startup

memory map

stack/heap model

runtime support calls

optional p-code interpreter

Stage C — native backend
Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.

Stage D — self-hosting subset
Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.

IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.

Why:

expressions map naturally

nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early

procedure calls are simpler

p-code history gives you many good precedents

A tiny IR might look like:

LDC k load constant

LD level, offset

ST level, offset

ADD SUB MUL DIV

EQ NE LT LE GT GE

JMP label

JZ label

CALL proc_id

RET

ENTER locals_size

LEAVE

LDI STI for indirect access

IDX for array indexing

FIELD offset

Then later map those to your 24-bit ISA.

The hardest design decisions you need early
These are the places where Pascal compilers get real:

1. Lexical nesting and static links
Pascal’s nested procedures are one of the biggest differences from C.

You need a decision for nonlocal access:

static link per frame

or display

or closure-like environment objects

For a first implementation, use static links. They are simpler and faithful to classic Pascal.

Each frame should contain:

return address

dynamic link

static link

locals

temporaries / spills if needed

2. Data layout on a 24-bit ISA
Decide up front:

size of integer

size of pointer

alignment rules

representation of boolean

char width

array bound representation

set representation

If your ISA is naturally 24-bit, I would strongly consider:

integer = signed 24-bit

pointer = 24-bit

boolean = 24-bit 0/1

char = 8-bit, but stored with clear load/store conventions

align aggregates to 24-bit word boundaries unless you have compelling byte-density reasons

Packed representations can wait.

3. Calling convention
Define this before codegen:

who pushes params

who cleans stack

how function result is returned

where static link goes

which registers are caller/callee saved

how large structs/records are passed

For simplicity:

caller pushes arguments left-to-right or right-to-left, but pick one and freeze it

caller also pushes static link

callee builds frame

return value in a dedicated register if scalar, hidden pointer if aggregate

4. Runtime model
You need a tiny Pascal runtime for:

startup

bounds-check failure

nil deref failure

division by zero

heap alloc/free if pointers are supported

I/O stubs

exit / halt

Keep it tiny and callable from generated code.

Debugger: how to design it from the start
You said you also want a debugger. Good. Add debug support immediately, not later.

I would design the compiler around a symbol-rich listing/debug model.

Emit these artifacts for every compile
token dump

AST dump

symbol table dump

frame layout report

IR listing

native assembly listing

source line map

procedure metadata

Runtime debugger features
At minimum:

single-step by source line

single-step by IR instruction

breakpoints by source line

show call stack

show current procedure

show locals/parameters

inspect static chain

inspect arbitrary memory

inspect globals

inspect heap blocks

show array bounds and current indices

trap on runtime errors

Best UI split
You already have Rust/Yew/WASM skills, so I would split it this way:

compiler/debug metadata generator in C

emulator instrumentation hooks in Rust or current emulator code

browser debugger UI in Yew/WASM

Views worth having:

source pane

current AST / scope pane

call stack pane

frame layout pane

registers pane

memory pane

static-chain visualization

disassembly pane

IR pane

For Pascal specifically, one killer feature is a scope-aware variable inspector:
show locals, parameters, globals, and nonlocals via static-link traversal.

Recommended project plan
Milestone 1 — define the language subset
Write these documents first:

language.md

runtime.md

abi.md

ir.md

debug-format.md

test-plan.md

This matters more than code at the start.

Milestone 2 — host interpreter for Pascal AST
Before codegen, you can interpret the AST directly for a tiny subset.

Pros:

fastest way to validate parser + semantics

easiest way to debug language rules

Cons:

not reusable for target execution

Still, it is often worth doing for the first 2–3 milestones.

Milestone 3 — p-code generator + host p-code VM
This becomes your semantic reference implementation.

Milestone 4 — target p-code VM on your 24-bit ISA
Now real Pascal programs run on your emulator/FPGA without needing native codegen yet.

Milestone 5 — native code generator
Target your 24-bit ISA directly.

Milestone 6 — debugger integration
Hook source lines, symbols, frames, and memory inspection into the emulator UI.

Milestone 7 — self-hosting subset
Rewrite enough of the compiler in Pascal to compile itself.

Open-source implementations worth studying
You asked specifically about liberal licenses.

Good for ideas / architecture
Pascal-P / Pascal-P5 is historically important and directly relevant because it is a portable p-code Pascal family. The Pascal-P5 project is presented as the fifth version of the Zurich Pascal-P compiler and emphasizes ISO 7185 compliance. 

Free Pascal is real, mature, and valuable to study conceptually, but its compiler itself is not BSD/MIT. Its FAQ says applications produced with its RTL are allowed to be proprietary because the RTL uses a modified LGPL exception, but that does not make the compiler source permissively licensed. 

Stanford Pascal is interesting because it still uses p-code plus an interpreter, and the repo says the compiler files are Pascal/P-Code while the interpreter is in C. I did not find an explicit permissive license on the GitHub page I checked, so I would treat it as “study carefully, license unclear until verified.” 

Small permissive educational repos
These are useful for architecture ideas, not for production-grade reuse:

JayPascal — MIT, Pascal compiler in C#. 

XDPC — MIT-0, small educational Pascal compiler with native x86 codegen. Very relevant as a “small compiler that directly emits native code” example, even though it targets DOS/x86. 

Qwerasdzxc/Pascal-Compiler — Apache-2.0, modular educational compiler with interpreter and C code generator. Architecturally useful because it separates lexer/parser/symbolizer/generator/runner. 

One caution: many permissively licensed Pascal repos on GitHub are student or educational compilers, not robust reference implementations. They are good for structure, testing ideas, and scope control, but not for blindly trusting semantics.

What I would personally avoid
For your project, I would avoid:

porting Turbo Pascal ideas too literally

implementing full ISO Pascal before subset stability

designing codegen before you define frame layout and static-scope access

adding real numbers early

building an optimizing compiler first

trying to self-host too soon

Suggested first deliverable
Your first usable milestone should be:

“Compile a small Pascal subset on the host into p-code, run it in a p-code VM on the emulator, and debug it with source-line stepping.”

That gives you:

proof of parsing

proof of semantic analysis

proof of runtime model

proof of debugger architecture

proof of target execution

before native code generation complexity arrives.

A concrete starting subset
I would start with exactly this grammar surface:

program

const

var

scalar integer/boolean

fixed arrays

procedures with parameters

function returning integer

begin/end

assignment

if/then/else

while/do

relational operators

arithmetic operators

writeln builtin

one-dimensional arrays only

That is enough to validate:

scopes

frames

calls

control flow

memory layout

debugger variable display

My recommendation in one sentence
Start with a clean-room Pascal-S/Pascal-P style subset in C, targeting a small p-code VM first, build the debugger metadata and frame model from day one, and only then add a native 24-bit code generator and eventual self-hosting.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete architecture.md + milestone plan + test matrix + debugger metadata format tailored to your 24-bit ISA.


So I want to split this project into layers.  A low-level p-code interpreter and possibly run-time.  How is memory typically managed in Pascal, malloc/free or gc or other?  Pascal would be the higher-level tooling to compile to the p-code.  Other languaged that compile to p-code would follow.


Thought for 8s
You want the project split into layers, with a low-level p-code machine/runtime first, and then Pascal as one front end targeting that machine, with room for other languages later. That is a good direction.

It gives you:

a stable execution substrate,

a semantic reference model before native codegen,

a reusable toolchain for multiple languages.

The key design question now is the runtime model: stack, heap, globals, code, metadata, and how dynamic memory works.

Short answer on Pascal memory management
Classic Pascal was not generally garbage-collected. The usual model was:

stack allocation for locals, parameters, temporaries

static/global storage for globals

heap allocation for dynamically created structures via new

explicit reclamation, usually via dispose, or sometimes effectively “free everything at program end”

That is still the cleanest model for your first implementation. Standard Pascal and mainstream descendants historically center on explicit heap management rather than GC. Free Pascal’s docs and wiki still document New/Dispose and GetMem/FreeMem style management, and its heap debugging support tracks allocation/deallocation rather than automatic tracing GC. 

So for your first p-code runtime, I would recommend:

no GC initially

explicit heap operations

optional region/arena reset at process end

later, optional reference counting or tracing GC as a runtime extension for other languages

Best layering for your project
I would split the system like this:

Layer 0 — target substrate
Your existing pieces:

assembler

emulator

FPGA hardware

low-level monitor/debug hooks

native calling convention for your 24-bit ISA

This layer should know nothing about Pascal.

Layer 1 — p-code VM / abstract machine
This becomes the real foundation.

It should define:

instruction set

value representation

activation record/frame layout

globals model

heap model

runtime traps

debug metadata hooks

Think of this as your language-neutral machine.

Historically, Pascal p-code systems such as UCSD Pascal used a stack machine model, with a shared stack for expression evaluation and procedure frames. That is exactly the right family of design here. 

Layer 2 — base runtime services
This is not “Pascal runtime” yet. It is the minimal substrate any front end can rely on:

heap allocator

string/array block helpers

bounds-check failure handler

nil-pointer failure handler

divide-by-zero handler

assertion/trap support

I/O hooks

debug symbol and source mapping support

Layer 3 — Pascal-specific runtime
Only put Pascal-specific semantics here:

new, dispose

read, write, writeln

Pascal file model, if you add it later

set operations, if not in the VM

conformant-array helpers, if you ever add them

Pascal string helpers, if you extend beyond packed char arrays

Layer 4 — front ends
Start with:

Pascal compiler → p-code

Later:

tiny BASIC → p-code

structured Forth-like front end → p-code

tiny Lisp → p-code

maybe a C subset → p-code

That is a strong architecture because the VM becomes your shared semantic platform.

How memory is usually organized in a Pascal system
A practical memory map for your p-code machine would be:

+--------------------+ high memory
| debugger scratch   |
| VM bookkeeping     |
+--------------------+
| heap               |  grows upward
|                    |
+--------------------+
| free space         |
+--------------------+
| stack              |  grows downward
| call frames        |
| eval stack         |
+--------------------+
| globals/statics    |
+--------------------+
| constants/literals |
+--------------------+
| p-code / bytecode  |
+--------------------+ low memory
That is a very natural fit for an emulator and FPGA target.

Separate or unified stack?
You have two choices:

Option A — one unified stack
Used for:

procedure frames

expression temporaries

This matches classic p-code machine design well. UCSD p-Machine is described as using one shared stack for both procedure stack frames and instruction operands. 

Pros

simple VM

fewer pointers/registers

historically faithful

Cons

debugger is slightly harder

expression stack corruption can damage frames if your VM is buggy

less convenient for multiple languages with different evaluation models

Option B — call stack + eval stack
Separate:

call stack for frames

eval stack for p-code operands

Pros

cleaner debugger

better invariants

easier to support multiple front ends later

easier to reason about stack depth separately from frame layout

Cons

slightly more VM state

a bit less “classic”

For your project, I would choose separate stacks unless you specifically want historical UCSD-like fidelity. It makes debugging much easier.

Recommended runtime memory model
For a first clean-room system, I would use four storage classes.

1. Globals/statics
Allocated once when program loads.

Use for:

global variables

typed constants that need storage

runtime state

2. Stack
Used for:

parameters

locals

temporaries

return address / dynamic link / static link

This is where Pascal naturally lives for ordinary code.

3. Heap
Used for:

pointers created by new

dynamic records

dynamic nodes for trees/lists

later, strings or managed arrays if you choose

4. Code/metadata space
Used for:

bytecode / p-code

literal pools

source line tables

symbol tables for debugger

What should heap allocation look like?
For Pascal first version: explicit allocation
I recommend these VM/runtime operations:

HEAP_ALLOC size -> ptr

HEAP_FREE ptr

HEAP_ZERO ptr, size

optional HEAP_REALLOC only if another language later wants it

Pascal front end then maps:

new(p) → allocate size of pointed-to type, assign pointer

dispose(p) → free and optionally nil the pointer

maybe mark/release as a nonstandard extension later

That gives you a small, understandable runtime.

Should the heap be malloc/free style?
Yes, for the first version.

Even if the source language says new/dispose, under the hood that is basically a typed wrapper around an allocator API equivalent to malloc/free. Free Pascal’s documentation explicitly distinguishes Pascal-level New/Dispose from lower-level GetMem/FreeMem, which supports this layered interpretation. 

So the layering can be:

VM allocator: raw blocks

Pascal runtime: typed heap ops on top

That is clean.

Should Pascal use GC?
Not initially.

Reasons:

not historically typical for classic Pascal

adds runtime and debugger complexity

muddies semantics while you are still validating the language

not needed to get real Pascal programs working

You can add GC later if another language benefits from it more.

Good heap strategies for your VM
For your hardware and emulator, here are the realistic options.

Option 1 — bump allocator only
Allocate by moving a heap pointer upward.

No free except “reset all”.

Pros

tiny

perfect for bring-up

very easy to debug

Cons

cannot support true dispose

leaks until program end

Good for:

compiler bring-up

tests

short-running tools

Option 2 — free-list allocator
Maintain block headers and a free list.

Pros

simple enough to implement

supports dispose

realistic for Pascal

Cons

fragmentation

coalescing logic

debugger must understand headers

This is the best first “real” allocator.

Option 3 — segregated size classes / slab style
Useful later for managed runtimes or object-heavy languages.

Pros

fast

predictable

Cons

more complexity

overkill initially

Option 4 — tracing GC
Mark-sweep, copying, or generational.

Pros

future-proof for Lisp-like languages

fewer leaks in managed languages

Cons

root scanning complexity

interaction with stack maps

debugger complexity

moving GC complicates raw pointer semantics

Not for version 1.

My recommendation for your first allocator
Implement two modes:

Bring-up mode
bump allocator

no free

reset heap between program runs

Real mode
free-list allocator with headers

block size

free/used bit

maybe next/prev free links

optional coalescing on free

This gives you:

fastest path to first success

smooth transition to a usable Pascal runtime

Pascal-specific semantic model for memory
Pascal usually feels cleaner than C because most ordinary objects are not heap allocated.

Typical pattern:

local records/arrays: stack

globals: static

only pointer-based structures: heap

That means your front end should aggressively prefer stack/static placement and use heap only when the source says so.

Suggested mapping
Variables
local scalar/record/array → frame offset

global scalar/record/array → global segment offset

Parameters
by value → copied into frame or parameter area

var parameter → address passed in

Functions/procedures
frame contains locals and bookkeeping

static link for nested scopes

Pointers
24-bit pointer to heap object or global/static object

nil = 0 is simplest, unless address 0 is valid in your architecture

Suggested p-code machine operations for memory
I would define VM instructions along these lines.

Addressing/storage
LDG offset — load global

STG offset — store global

LDL offset — load local

STL offset — store local

LDA_LOCAL offset — push address of local

LDA_GLOBAL offset — push address of global

LOAD_IND size

STORE_IND size

Frame/nonlocal access
FOLLOW_STATIC depth

LDN depth, offset

STN depth, offset

LDA_NONLOCAL depth, offset

Heap
ALLOC size

FREE

MEMZERO size

MEMCOPY size

Checking
CHK_BOUNDS lo, hi

CHK_NIL

CHK_TAG if you later add tagged objects

TRAP code

Aggregate helpers
INDEX elem_size, low_bound

FIELD offset

This instruction set can support Pascal cleanly and still be reusable for other languages.

Should the runtime be Pascal-specific or generic?
You said other languages may follow. So keep the lower runtime generic.

Generic VM/runtime should own
raw allocation

raw memory movement

stack/frame model

trap handling

debug hooks

primitive I/O abstraction

Pascal front end/runtime should own
static typing rules

new / dispose

var parameter semantics

nested procedure semantics

Pascal built-ins

Pascal standard type semantics

That split lets you later add languages with different memory models.

For example:

Pascal → explicit allocation

Lisp → GC heap

BASIC → dynamic strings + simple reference counting

Forth-like language → manual dictionary arena

Same VM, different front-end/runtime layers.

If other languages will follow, what memory model should the VM expose?
A good neutral model is:

VM object kinds
Not source-language-visible, just runtime categories:

raw block

string block

array block

record/object block

closure/env block

runtime descriptor block

You do not need this all up front, but it is helpful to reserve the concept.

Minimal neutral API
At the VM/runtime boundary:

alloc raw block

free raw block

read/write primitive

copy/fill

query size

optional type tag in header

optional finalizer hook later

That is enough to host both manual-memory and managed-memory languages.

Debugger implications
If memory matters, design the allocator and frames so the debugger can introspect them.

Heap block header suggestion
Each allocated block could have:

struct HeapHeader {
    u24 size_words;
    u24 flags;
    u24 type_id;      // optional
    u24 prev_or_next; // depending on allocator design
}
Useful flags:

allocated/free

Pascal object

array

string

record

internal runtime block

maybe “poisoned/freed”

Then your debugger can show:

live heap blocks

free blocks

leaks on exit

block ownership/type

double free attempts

use-after-free if you poison blocks in debug mode

Frame record suggestion
Each call frame should expose:

return PC

previous frame pointer / dynamic link

static link

procedure id

locals base

eval stack base snapshot

That makes nested Pascal debugging much easier.

A practical staged plan
Phase 1 — define the VM first
Write documents for:

vm-isa.md

vm-memory.md

vm-abi.md

vm-debug.md

Do this before writing much code.

Phase 2 — build a tiny VM with no Pascal yet
Support:

constants

arithmetic

branches

calls

locals/globals

heap alloc/free

traps

line stepping

Then hand-write small p-code tests.

Phase 3 — add Pascal subset front end
Compile a tiny subset to p-code:

integers

booleans

assignment

if

while

procedures

arrays

records

new / dispose

Phase 4 — add debugger
Debugger should show:

p-code instruction stream

current line

frame chain

static chain

locals

globals

heap blocks

Phase 5 — let a second language target the same VM
This will force you to keep the VM generic rather than accidentally baking Pascal into it.

Concrete recommendation on memory policy
If I were designing this for your 24-bit platform, I would do this:

VM v1
separate call stack and eval stack

globals segment

heap with bump allocator in bring-up mode

free-list allocator in normal mode

no GC

explicit traps

pointer is 24-bit

debug headers on every heap block in debug builds

Pascal v1
no GC

new / dispose

stack for locals

globals for statics

heap only for pointers

optional leak report at process exit

runtime bounds checks enabled by default

Later multi-language extension
Add optional alternate heap managers:

manual heap

reference-counted heap

tracing GC heap

and let each front end choose one.

That avoids forcing Pascal into a GC design it does not need, while still leaving room for Lisp-like or scripting languages later.

Bottom line
For your layered architecture, I would treat Pascal as a typed front end over a generic p-code VM, and I would use explicit heap management, not GC, for the first runtime.

That means:

stack for locals and temporaries

globals for static storage

heap for pointer-based dynamic objects

new / dispose at the Pascal level

allocator API underneath that is basically malloc/free in spirit

GC only later, as an optional runtime mode for other languages

That is historically aligned with Pascal, technically simpler, and a better base for a debugger and for additional languages. 

I can turn this into a concrete vm-memory.md and pcode-opcodes.md draft next.


Okay, in order to write tests, I'd like to introduce a p-code-assembler. Does tha make sense?  It would be a subset of Pascal not sharing Pascal idioms, but enough to create Hello World, toggle LEDs, read/write UART.   It would have a simple calling convention, not support typing per-sed, or complex data structures.  The target ISA is integer only, so no floating point.  Does this make sense?

Yes — that makes a lot of sense.

In fact, for your project I think a p-code assembler is one of the best early moves, because it lets you separate three problems that otherwise get tangled together:

VM design

runtime / calling convention design

Pascal front-end correctness

If you skip the assembler, every early failure is ambiguous:

is the VM wrong?

is the runtime wrong?

is the code generator wrong?

is the parser wrong?

is the Pascal semantic analysis wrong?

A p-code assembler gives you a way to test the VM and runtime directly, with tiny hand-written programs.

Restating the architecture
You are proposing a layered system like this:

Layer A — p-code VM
Executes a small instruction set.

Layer B — p-code assembler
Human-writable source language for tests, demos, low-level runtime bring-up.

Layer C — runtime library / monitor bindings
UART, LED, memory, traps, maybe stack/heap helpers.

Layer D — Pascal compiler
Compiles Pascal into p-code.

Layer E — later front ends
Other languages also target the same p-code.

That is a very good architecture.

The p-code assembler becomes for your VM what assembly is for a real ISA:

a precise test tool

a bootstrap tool

a debugger input language

a way to write tiny runtime pieces before Pascal is ready

Why this is a strong idea
1. It gives you direct VM tests
You will want tests like:

push constants, add, print result

branch on zero

call/return

access locals/globals

follow static/dynamic links if you have them

allocate/free heap block

write UART

toggle LED

bounds trap

nil trap

These are much easier to express in a tiny assembly-like language than in Pascal.

2. It lets you bootstrap the runtime before Pascal exists
Before the Pascal compiler works, you still need:

startup

I/O shims

debug/trap handlers

maybe memory helpers

A p-code assembler lets you write these as VM-level programs or modules.

3. It clarifies the calling convention early
You said “simple calling convention.” Good.

The assembler forces you to define:

what a call frame looks like

argument passing

return values

local storage reservation

stack cleanup rules

That discipline helps both the VM and Pascal backend.

4. It gives you reproducible debugger cases
Debugger bring-up is hard if every test comes from a buggy compiler.

With p-asm, you can hand-write:

a three-function call chain

a recursive countdown

a loop with locals

a UART output routine

a deliberate trap

Then verify stepping, breakpoints, stack display, and memory inspection.

My recommendation: make it explicitly not Pascal
I agree with your instinct that it should not share Pascal idioms.

Do not try to make it “tiny Pascal.”

Make it look like a machine-oriented textual form.

That keeps the layers clean:

Pascal is a source language

p-asm is a target-language authoring tool

That distinction is healthy.

What the p-code assembler should be
Think of it as:

untyped or lightly typed

integer-only

explicit control flow

explicit loads/stores

explicit call frames

explicit memory operations

labels

directives for globals, constants, strings, imports/exports

It should feel closer to:

bytecode assembly

threaded-code assembly

simple VM macro assembly

than to Pascal or C.

Good scope for v1
You want enough to write:

Hello World

LED toggle

UART read/write

simple loops

simple function calls

stack and global tests

That suggests a very small feature set.

Source language features
labels

procedures/functions

constants

string literals

integer literals

comments

directives for globals/data

instructions

optional macros later

No need initially for
type declarations

structs

arrays in source syntax

records

source-level pointers

floating point

polymorphism

expression parsing beyond maybe trivial immediate syntax

A good design principle
The assembler should expose the VM’s semantics almost 1:1.

So if the VM has an instruction like:

PUSH 42

LOADG uart_status

JZ loop

CALL putc

RET

then the assembler should map directly to that.

Avoid “convenient” high-level sugar too early.

You want this tool mainly for:

clarity

testing

determinism

not productivity.

One key decision: stack machine vs explicit locals model
Since this is p-code, the natural design is a stack-oriented assembler.

Example style:

proc main
    push 72
    call putc
    push 105
    call putc
    push 10
    call putc
    halt
endproc
But for debugging and maintainability, I recommend you also support named local slots or explicit frame offsets.

For example:

proc blink locals 1
loop:
    push 1
    out led
    call delay
    push 0
    out led
    call delay
    jmp loop
endproc
or, more machine-like:

proc blink 1
loop:
    push 1
    storeg LED_PORT
    call delay
    push 0
    storeg LED_PORT
    call delay
    jmp loop
endproc
That is enough.

Suggested minimum p-code VM model
For your custom integer-only machine, I would keep the VM itself integer-only too.

Core VM state
pc — p-code instruction pointer

fp — frame pointer

sp — eval stack pointer

gp — globals base, or implicit globals segment

maybe hp — heap pointer if heap exists now

Core data model
one scalar word type: signed/unsigned 24-bit integer value

addresses are also word-sized

byte loads/stores handled explicitly if needed for UART and strings

That is enough for quite a while.

Suggested instruction categories
Stack / constants
push <imm>

dup

drop

swap

over

Arithmetic / logic
add

sub

mul

div

mod

neg

and

or

xor

not

Comparison
eq

ne

lt

le

gt

ge

These can push 0/1.

Control flow
jmp <label>

jz <label>

jnz <label>

call <proc>

ret

halt

trap <code>

Frame/local/global access
enter <nlocals>

leave

loadl <offset>

storel <offset>

loadg <symbol>

storeg <symbol>

addrg <symbol>

addrl <offset>

Indirect memory
load

store

loadb

storeb

Optional runtime
alloc <size>

free

sys <id> or hostcall <id>

You may not need heap yet, but having the slot reserved is useful.

Calling convention recommendation
Since this is for bring-up and testing, make the convention brutally simple.

Suggested convention
arguments are pushed on the eval stack

call foo

callee uses enter nlocals

arguments become addressable relative to frame pointer

return value is pushed on the eval stack before ret

caller removes args if needed, or callee does — pick one and stick to it

For simplicity, I would choose:

Better v1 convention
caller pushes args

call foo

callee reads args at known negative offsets from fp

callee leaves one return value on the eval stack, or none for procedures

callee pops its frame only

arguments are cleaned by callee or encoded in ret n

A nice compact form is:

ret 0 for no args

ret 2 meaning pop 2 args and return

or retv 2 meaning return value + pop 2 args

But that may complicate the VM slightly.

A simpler alternative:

keep call frames separate from eval stack

copy args into frame slots on call

That is cleaner but a bit more machinery.

For early work, either is fine. If you expect lots of debugging, a separate frame area is easier to reason about.

For your use case, I would recommend two abstractions for I/O
Since you want UART and LED access, decide whether p-code talks to:

Option A — VM-level abstract devices
Example:

sys PUTC

sys GETC

sys LED_WRITE

Pros

portable across host interpreter and real hardware

easy to test

easier debugger integration

Cons

less “metal”

Option B — memory-mapped addresses
Example:

push UART_DATA

push 65

storeb

Pros

closer to the hardware

fewer abstractions

Cons

less portable

awkward for hosted tests

slightly harder to evolve

Best answer: support both
Use memory-mapped addresses in the runtime if you want, but also provide small VM/syscall abstractions for common tasks.

For example:

putc implemented as runtime p-asm

inside it, either call sys PUTC on host, or do memory-mapped UART on target

That keeps the assembler small and the runtime portable.

Hello World / LED / UART are perfect early test cases
These are exactly the right kinds of tests.

Hello World tests
Validates:

string/data section handling

loops

loadb/storeb

call/return

UART output

LED toggle tests
Validates:

MMIO writes

loops

delay call structure

integration with emulator/FPGA

UART read/write
Validates:

polling loops

conditional branching

MMIO reads and writes

byte operations

Those will shake out most VM bugs quickly.

A concrete source-language sketch
Something like this would work well:

.const UART_DATA   0xFF0100
.const UART_STATUS 0xFF0101
.const LED_PORT    0xFF0200

.proc putc
    ; arg0 at fp-1
wait:
    push UART_STATUS
    loadb
    push 1
    and
    jz wait

    push UART_DATA
    loadarg 0
    storeb
    ret 1
.end

.proc puts
loop:
    loadarg 0
    loadb
    dup
    jz done
    call putc
    loadarg 0
    push 1
    add
    storearg 0
    jmp loop
done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

.data msg "Hello, world!\n", 0

.proc main
    push msg
    call puts
    halt
.end
I am not saying this exact syntax is best, only that this level of abstraction is appropriate.

One subtle point: typed vs untyped
You said “not support typing per se.”

That is right for the assembler source language. But the VM still needs operational widths.

Even in an “untyped” p-code assembler, you will still need distinctions like:

word load/store

byte load/store

signed vs unsigned compare, maybe

address vs integer conventions, even if both are same-sized

So the source language can be untyped, but the instruction set should still be explicit enough to avoid ambiguity.

For example:

load = word

loadb = byte

lt = signed compare

ltu = unsigned compare if needed later

This will matter for UART, chars, string traversal, and array/string runtime work.

Should the assembler include macros?
Not initially.

For v1, I would keep it extremely small:

labels

constants

procedures

data/string directives

instructions

Then after the VM is stable, add:

.macro

.include

conditional assembly

symbolic frame slot aliases

Macros are helpful later, but they can hide VM design problems early.

What to test first
I would define a staged test suite.

Stage 1 — pure VM instruction tests
push/add/sub

dup/drop/swap

compare/branch

simple halt

Stage 2 — procedure tests
one call

nested calls

recursion

return value

locals

Stage 3 — memory tests
globals

indirect load/store

byte load/store

string walk

Stage 4 — device tests
UART output

UART polling input

LED on/off

blink loop

Stage 5 — error tests
bad opcode

stack underflow

invalid memory access

explicit trap

These tests should exist before Pascal codegen starts producing real programs.

This also helps your future debugger design
A p-code assembler pairs naturally with a debugger.

You can support debug info at two levels:

Level 1 — instruction/label level
current pc

current proc

label names

stack contents

frame contents

Level 2 — source mapping
p-asm source line to p-code address

later Pascal source line to p-code address

That means your debugger can be useful before Pascal exists.

This is a big win.

One thing I would avoid
I would avoid making the p-code assembler too much like a “portable assembly language for the target ISA.”

It should target the VM, not become a second real ISA project.

Otherwise you risk re-creating a complicated lower-level compiler target and losing the simplicity benefit.

So keep it:

stack-based

compact

procedural

explicit

test-friendly

My recommendation
Yes, introduce a p-code assembler now.

Make it:

machine-like, not Pascal-like

integer-only

stack-oriented

simple calling convention

labels + directives + instructions

enough for:

Hello World

LED toggle

UART I/O

loops

calls

globals

byte/word memory access

And use it for:

VM bring-up

runtime bring-up

debugger bring-up

regression tests

hand-authored demos

That is a very strong foundation for Pascal and later languages.

Recommended next concrete artifacts
Write these first:

pvm.md
VM state

memory layout

stack model

traps

pasm.md
syntax

directives

labels

procedures

calling convention

opcode-table.md
every opcode

operands

stack effect

semantics

trap conditions

tests/
hand-written .pasm test programs

expected stdout / memory / trap results

The best next step is probably to define the actual pasm syntax and the first 20–30 opcodes.

1→
     2→
     3→
     4→
     5→How would I implement Pascal from scratch for a custom 24-bit ISA?  I have a working C compiler and Assembler (and emulator and FPGA hardware).  Was Pascal bootstapped in assembler or C and then written in Pascal?  Are there open source implementations with liberal licenses (BSD, MIT) that I can look at?  I do not want to port from a 16-bit or 32-bit system.  I prefer to write-from-scratch, clean-room implementation, possibly using already-written Pascal souce examples for testing my compiler.  I also want a debugger.  How should I start?  
     6→
     7→
     8→Thought for 36s
     9→Given your constraints, I would not start by trying to build a self-hosting Pascal compiler immediately.
    10→
    11→You already have the right base:
    12→
    13→working assembler
    14→
    15→working C compiler
    16→
    17→emulator + FPGA target
    18→
    19→desire for a clean-room implementation
    20→
    21→debugger requirement from day one
    22→
    23→That points to a practical architecture:
    24→
    25→Recommended architecture
    26→Build Pascal in three stages:
    27→
    28→Pascal front end in C
    29→
    30→lexer
    31→
    32→parser
    33→
    34→AST
    35→
    36→symbol tables
    37→
    38→type checker
    39→
    40→simple error recovery
    41→
    42→Portable intermediate form
    43→
    44→either a small stack VM / p-code
    45→
    46→or a very small typed IR of your own
    47→
    48→Back ends
    49→
    50→first: interpreter for the IR/p-code on the host
    51→
    52→second: native code generator for your 24-bit ISA
    53→
    54→later: optional self-hosting compiler in Pascal
    55→
    56→That is the lowest-risk path because it lets you validate semantics before you fight register allocation, calling conventions, and codegen bugs.
    57→
    58→Historically, how Pascal was bootstrapped
    59→The earliest Pascal compiler was not originally bootstrapped in assembler in the way many people assume. Niklaus Wirth’s first FORTRAN-based attempt was unsuccessful; the successful implementation was done in a C-like language called Scallop and then translated by hand into Pascal for bootstrapping. Many later Pascal compilers became self-hosting. A famous exception is GNU Pascal, which was written in C. Turbo Pascal, much later, was famously written in assembly for speed and size. 
    60→
    61→So the historical pattern is roughly:
    62→
    63→prototype in another language
    64→
    65→get a working compiler
    66→
    67→rewrite or re-express the compiler in Pascal
    68→
    69→use the previous compiler to compile the new compiler
    70→
    71→That is exactly the path I would recommend for your ISA as well.
    72→
    73→What to target first: native code or p-code?
    74→For your situation, I would start with a Pascal-S / Pascal-P style subset and a p-code-like intermediate machine.
    75→
    76→Why:
    77→
    78→Pascal semantics are nontrivial enough already: nested procedures, static scoping, sets, records, packed/unpacked data, file types, pass-by-reference, variant records, etc.
    79→
    80→your ISA is 24-bit, which means you will be making many nonstandard representation decisions anyway
    81→
    82→a p-code interpreter gives you a correctness oracle before native codegen
    83→
    84→Historically, Pascal-P was created specifically as a compiler porting kit: Pascal source compiled to p-code for a virtual stack machine, then interpreted on each target. That was done to make Pascal portable. 
    85→
    86→For a custom ISA, that strategy is still excellent.
    87→
    88→What subset should you implement first
    89→Do not begin with full ISO Pascal.
    90→
    91→Begin with a clean educational subset:
    92→
    93→Phase 0: expression language
    94→integers only
    95→
    96→constants
    97→
    98→variables
    99→
   100→assignment
   101→
   102→arithmetic
   103→
   104→comparisons
   105→
   106→if, while
   107→
   108→begin ... end
   109→
   110→writeln-like builtin for debugging
   111→
   112→Phase 1: real Pascal block structure
   113→program
   114→
   115→declarations
   116→
   117→scalar types: integer, boolean, char
   118→
   119→arrays with fixed bounds
   120→
   121→records
   122→
   123→procedures/functions
   124→
   125→lexical nesting
   126→
   127→local variables
   128→
   129→pass-by-value and var parameters
   130→
   131→Phase 2: features that force runtime design
   132→strings or packed char arrays
   133→
   134→sets
   135→
   136→pointers
   137→
   138→heap
   139→
   140→files
   141→
   142→case statements
   143→
   144→standard procedures/functions
   145→
   146→Phase 3: optional/full language
   147→ISO-ish cleanup
   148→
   149→conformance tests
   150→
   151→self-hosting subset
   152→
   153→For your first compiler, avoid:
   154→
   155→packed types
   156→
   157→files
   158→
   159→variant records
   160→
   161→conformant arrays
   162→
   163→procedural parameters
   164→
   165→goto
   166→
   167→real numbers
   168→
   169→Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.
   170→
   171→Clean-room strategy
   172→Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.
   173→
   174→A good clean-room posture is:
   175→
   176→read language reports and public docs
   177→
   178→study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers
   179→
   180→do not copy implementation code
   181→
   182→write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen
   183→
   184→use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs
   185→
   186→That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.
   187→
   188→Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
   189→Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:
   190→
   191→Stage A — host-side compiler in C
   192→Run on your current host machine.
   193→
   194→Outputs either:
   195→
   196→textual p-code / bytecode
   197→
   198→or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA
   199→
   200→This lets you iterate quickly.
   201→
   202→Stage B — target runtime
   203→On your ISA:
   204→
   205→tiny startup
   206→
   207→memory map
   208→
   209→stack/heap model
   210→
   211→runtime support calls
   212→
   213→optional p-code interpreter
   214→
   215→Stage C — native backend
   216→Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.
   217→
   218→Stage D — self-hosting subset
   219→Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.
   220→
   221→IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
   222→For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.
   223→
   224→Why:
   225→
   226→expressions map naturally
   227→
   228→nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early
   229→
   230→procedure calls are simpler
   231→
   232→p-code history gives you many good precedents
   233→
   234→A tiny IR might look like:
   235→
   236→LDC k load constant
   237→
   238→LD level, offset
   239→
   240→ST level, offset
   241→
   242→ADD SUB MUL DIV
   243→
   244→EQ NE LT LE GT GE
   245→
   246→JMP label
   247→
   248→JZ label
   249→
   250→CALL proc_id
   251→
   252→RET
   253→
   254→ENTER locals_size
   255→
   256→LEAVE
   257→
   258→LDI STI for indirect access
   259→
   260→IDX for array indexing
   261→
   262→FIELD offset
   263→
   264→Then later map those to your 24-bit ISA.
   265→
   266→The hardest design decisions you need early
   267→These are the places where Pascal compilers get real:
   268→
   269→1. Lexical nesting and static links
   270→Pascal’s nested procedures are one of the biggest differences from C.
   271→
   272→You need a decision for nonlocal access:
   273→
   274→static link per frame
   275→
   276→or display
   277→
   278→or closure-like environment objects
   279→
   280→For a first implementation, use static links. They are simpler and faithful to classic Pascal.
   281→
   282→Each frame should contain:
   283→
   284→return address
   285→
   286→dynamic link
   287→
   288→static link
   289→
   290→locals
   291→
   292→temporaries / spills if needed
   293→
   294→2. Data layout on a 24-bit ISA
   295→Decide up front:
   296→
   297→size of integer
   298→
   299→size of pointer
   300→
   301→alignment rules
   302→
   303→representation of boolean
   304→
   305→char width
   306→
   307→array bound representation
   308→
   309→set representation
   310→
   311→If your ISA is naturally 24-bit, I would strongly consider:
   312→
   313→integer = signed 24-bit
   314→
   315→pointer = 24-bit
   316→
   317→boolean = 24-bit 0/1
   318→
   319→char = 8-bit, but stored with clear load/store conventions
   320→
   321→align aggregates to 24-bit word boundaries unless you have compelling byte-density reasons
   322→
   323→Packed representations can wait.
   324→
   325→3. Calling convention
   326→Define this before codegen:
   327→
   328→who pushes params
   329→
   330→who cleans stack
   331→
   332→how function result is returned
   333→
   334→where static link goes
   335→
   336→which registers are caller/callee saved
   337→
   338→how large structs/records are passed
   339→
   340→For simplicity:
   341→
   342→caller pushes arguments left-to-right or right-to-left, but pick one and freeze it
   343→
   344→caller also pushes static link
   345→
   346→callee builds frame
   347→
   348→return value in a dedicated register if scalar, hidden pointer if aggregate
   349→
   350→4. Runtime model
   351→You need a tiny Pascal runtime for:
   352→
   353→startup
   354→
   355→bounds-check failure
   356→
   357→nil deref failure
   358→
   359→division by zero
   360→
   361→heap alloc/free if pointers are supported
   362→
   363→I/O stubs
   364→
   365→exit / halt
   366→
   367→Keep it tiny and callable from generated code.
   368→
   369→Debugger: how to design it from the start
   370→You said you also want a debugger. Good. Add debug support immediately, not later.
   371→
   372→I would design the compiler around a symbol-rich listing/debug model.
   373→
   374→Emit these artifacts for every compile
   375→token dump
   376→
   377→AST dump
   378→
   379→symbol table dump
   380→
   381→frame layout report
   382→
   383→IR listing
   384→
   385→native assembly listing
   386→
   387→source line map
   388→
   389→procedure metadata
   390→
   391→Runtime debugger features
   392→At minimum:
   393→
   394→single-step by source line
   395→
   396→single-step by IR instruction
   397→
   398→breakpoints by source line
   399→
   400→show call stack
   401→
   402→show current procedure
   403→
   404→show locals/parameters
   405→
   406→inspect static chain
   407→
   408→inspect arbitrary memory
   409→
   410→inspect globals
   411→
   412→inspect heap blocks
   413→
   414→show array bounds and current indices
   415→
   416→trap on runtime errors
   417→
   418→Best UI split
   419→You already have Rust/Yew/WASM skills, so I would split it this way:
   420→
   421→compiler/debug metadata generator in C
   422→
   423→emulator instrumentation hooks in Rust or current emulator code
   424→
   425→browser debugger UI in Yew/WASM
   426→
   427→Views worth having:
   428→
   429→source pane
   430→
   431→current AST / scope pane
   432→
   433→call stack pane
   434→
   435→frame layout pane
   436→
   437→registers pane
   438→
   439→memory pane
   440→
   441→static-chain visualization
   442→
   443→disassembly pane
   444→
   445→IR pane
   446→
   447→For Pascal specifically, one killer feature is a scope-aware variable inspector:
   448→show locals, parameters, globals, and nonlocals via static-link traversal.
   449→
   450→Recommended project plan
   451→Milestone 1 — define the language subset
   452→Write these documents first:
   453→
   454→language.md
   455→
   456→runtime.md
   457→
   458→abi.md
   459→
   460→ir.md
   461→
   462→debug-format.md
   463→
   464→test-plan.md
   465→
   466→This matters more than code at the start.
   467→
   468→Milestone 2 — host interpreter for Pascal AST
   469→Before codegen, you can interpret the AST directly for a tiny subset.
   470→
   471→Pros:
   472→
   473→fastest way to validate parser + semantics
   474→
   475→easiest way to debug language rules
   476→
   477→Cons:
   478→
   479→not reusable for target execution
   480→
   481→Still, it is often worth doing for the first 2–3 milestones.
   482→
   483→Milestone 3 — p-code generator + host p-code VM
   484→This becomes your semantic reference implementation.
   485→
   486→Milestone 4 — target p-code VM on your 24-bit ISA
   487→Now real Pascal programs run on your emulator/FPGA without needing native codegen yet.
   488→
   489→Milestone 5 — native code generator
   490→Target your 24-bit ISA directly.
   491→
   492→Milestone 6 — debugger integration
   493→Hook source lines, symbols, frames, and memory inspection into the emulator UI.
   494→
   495→Milestone 7 — self-hosting subset
   496→Rewrite enough of the compiler in Pascal to compile itself.
   497→
   498→Open-source implementations worth studying
   499→You asked specifically about liberal licenses.
   500→
   501→Good for ideas / architecture
   502→Pascal-P / Pascal-P5 is historically important and directly relevant because it is a portable p-code Pascal family. The Pascal-P5 project is presented as the fifth version of the Zurich Pascal-P compiler and emphasizes ISO 7185 compliance. 
   503→
   504→Free Pascal is real, mature, and valuable to study conceptually, but its compiler itself is not BSD/MIT. Its FAQ says applications produced with its RTL are allowed to be proprietary because the RTL uses a modified LGPL exception, but that does not make the compiler source permissively licensed. 
   505→
   506→Stanford Pascal is interesting because it still uses p-code plus an interpreter, and the repo says the compiler files are Pascal/P-Code while the interpreter is in C. I did not find an explicit permissive license on the GitHub page I checked, so I would treat it as “study carefully, license unclear until verified.” 
   507→
   508→Small permissive educational repos
   509→These are useful for architecture ideas, not for production-grade reuse:
   510→
   511→JayPascal — MIT, Pascal compiler in C#. 
   512→
   513→XDPC — MIT-0, small educational Pascal compiler with native x86 codegen. Very relevant as a “small compiler that directly emits native code” example, even though it targets DOS/x86. 
   514→
   515→Qwerasdzxc/Pascal-Compiler — Apache-2.0, modular educational compiler with interpreter and C code generator. Architecturally useful because it separates lexer/parser/symbolizer/generator/runner. 
   516→
   517→One caution: many permissively licensed Pascal repos on GitHub are student or educational compilers, not robust reference implementations. They are good for structure, testing ideas, and scope control, but not for blindly trusting semantics.
   518→
   519→What I would personally avoid
   520→For your project, I would avoid:
   521→
   522→porting Turbo Pascal ideas too literally
   523→
   524→implementing full ISO Pascal before subset stability
   525→
   526→designing codegen before you define frame layout and static-scope access
   527→
   528→adding real numbers early
   529→
   530→building an optimizing compiler first
   531→
   532→trying to self-host too soon
   533→

   536→
   537→“Compile a small Pascal subset on the host into p-code, run it in a p-code VM on the emulator, and debug it with source-line stepping.”
   538→
   539→That gives you:
   540→
   541→proof of parsing
   542→
   543→proof of semantic analysis
   544→

   546→
   547→proof of debugger architecture
   548→
   549→proof of target execution
   550→
   551→before native code generation complexity arrives.

   600→My recommendation in one sentence
   601→Start with a clean-room Pascal-S/Pascal-P style subset in C, targeting a small p-code VM first, build the debugger metadata and frame model from day one, and only then add a native 24-bit code generator and eventual self-hosting.
   602→
   603→If you want, I can turn this into a concrete architecture.md + milestone plan + test matrix + debugger metadata format tailored to your 24-bit ISA.
   604→
   605→
   606→So I want to split this project into layers.  A low-level p-code interpreter and possibly run-time.  How is memory typically managed in Pascal, malloc/free or gc or other?  Pascal would be the higher-level tooling to compile to the p-code.  Other languaged that compile to p-code would follow.
   607→
   608→
   609→Thought for 8s
   610→You want the project split into layers, with a low-level p-code machine/runtime first, and then Pascal as one front end targeting that machine, with room for other languages later. That is a good direction.
   611→
   612→It gives you:
   613→
   614→a stable execution substrate,
   615→
   616→a semantic reference model before native codegen,
   617→
   618→a reusable toolchain for multiple languages.
   619→
   620→The key design question now is the runtime model: stack, heap, globals, code, metadata, and how dynamic memory works.
   621→
   622→Short answer on Pascal memory management
   623→Classic Pascal was not generally garbage-collected. The usual model was:
   624→
   625→stack allocation for locals, parameters, temporaries
   626→
   627→static/global storage for globals
   628→
   629→heap allocation for dynamically created structures via new
   630→
   631→explicit reclamation, usually via dispose, or sometimes effectively “free everything at program end”
   632→
   633→That is still the cleanest model for your first implementation. Standard Pascal and mainstream descendants historically center on explicit heap management rather than GC. Free Pascal’s docs and wiki still document New/Dispose and GetMem/FreeMem style management, and its heap debugging support tracks allocation/deallocation rather than automatic tracing GC. 
   634→
   635→So for your first p-code runtime, I would recommend:
   636→
   637→no GC initially
   638→
   639→explicit heap operations
   640→
   641→optional region/arena reset at process end
   642→
   643→later, optional reference counting or tracing GC as a runtime extension for other languages
   644→
   645→Best layering for your project
   646→I would split the system like this:
   647→
   648→Layer 0 — target substrate
   649→Your existing pieces:
   650→
   651→assembler
   652→
   653→emulator
   654→
   655→FPGA hardware
   656→
   657→low-level monitor/debug hooks
   658→
   659→native calling convention for your 24-bit ISA
   660→
   661→This layer should know nothing about Pascal.
   662→
   663→Layer 1 — p-code VM / abstract machine
   664→This becomes the real foundation.
   665→
   666→It should define:
   667→
   668→instruction set
   669→
   670→value representation
   671→
   672→activation record/frame layout
   673→
   674→globals model
   675→
   676→heap model
   677→
   678→runtime traps
   679→
   680→debug metadata hooks
   681→
   682→Think of this as your language-neutral machine.
   683→
   684→Historically, Pascal p-code systems such as UCSD Pascal used a stack machine model, with a shared stack for expression evaluation and procedure frames. That is exactly the right family of design here. 
   685→
   686→Layer 2 — base runtime services
   687→This is not “Pascal runtime” yet. It is the minimal substrate any front end can rely on:
   688→
   689→heap allocator
   690→
   691→string/array block helpers
   692→
   693→bounds-check failure handler
   694→
   695→nil-pointer failure handler
   696→
   697→divide-by-zero handler
   698→
   699→assertion/trap support
   700→
   701→I/O hooks
   702→
   703→debug symbol and source mapping support
   704→
   705→Layer 3 — Pascal-specific runtime
   706→Only put Pascal-specific semantics here:
   707→
   708→new, dispose
   709→
   710→read, write, writeln
   711→
   712→Pascal file model, if you add it later
   713→
   714→set operations, if not in the VM
   715→
   716→conformant-array helpers, if you ever add them
   717→
   718→Pascal string helpers, if you extend beyond packed char arrays
   719→
   720→Layer 4 — front ends
   721→Start with:
   722→
   723→Pascal compiler → p-code
   724→
   725→Later:
   726→
   727→tiny BASIC → p-code
   728→
   729→structured Forth-like front end → p-code
   730→
   731→tiny Lisp → p-code
   732→
   733→maybe a C subset → p-code
   734→
   735→That is a strong architecture because the VM becomes your shared semantic platform.
   736→
   737→How memory is usually organized in a Pascal system
   738→A practical memory map for your p-code machine would be:
   739→
   740→+--------------------+ high memory
   741→| debugger scratch   |
   742→| VM bookkeeping     |
   743→+--------------------+
   744→| heap               |  grows upward
   745→|                    |
   746→+--------------------+
   747→| free space         |
   748→+--------------------+
   749→| stack              |  grows downward
   750→| call frames        |
   751→| eval stack         |
   752→+--------------------+
   753→| globals/statics    |
   754→+--------------------+
   755→| constants/literals |
   756→+--------------------+
   757→| p-code / bytecode  |
   758→+--------------------+ low memory
   759→That is a very natural fit for an emulator and FPGA target.
   760→
   761→Separate or unified stack?
   762→You have two choices:
   763→
   764→Option A — one unified stack
   765→Used for:
   766→
   767→procedure frames
   768→
   769→expression temporaries
   770→
   771→This matches classic p-code machine design well. UCSD p-Machine is described as using one shared stack for both procedure stack frames and instruction operands. 
   772→
   773→Pros
   774→
   775→simple VM
   776→
   777→fewer pointers/registers
   778→
   779→historically faithful
   780→
   781→Cons
   782→
   783→debugger is slightly harder
   784→
   785→expression stack corruption can damage frames if your VM is buggy
   786→
   787→less convenient for multiple languages with different evaluation models
   788→
   789→Option B — call stack + eval stack
   790→Separate:
   791→
   792→call stack for frames
   793→
   794→eval stack for p-code operands
   795→
   796→Pros
   797→
   798→cleaner debugger
   799→
   800→better invariants
   801→
   802→easier to support multiple front ends later
   803→
   804→easier to reason about stack depth separately from frame layout
   805→
   806→Cons
   807→
   808→slightly more VM state
   809→
   810→a bit less “classic”
   811→
   812→For your project, I would choose separate stacks unless you specifically want historical UCSD-like fidelity. It makes debugging much easier.
   813→
   814→Recommended runtime memory model
   815→For a first clean-room system, I would use four storage classes.
   816→
   817→1. Globals/statics
   818→Allocated once when program loads.
   819→
   820→Use for:
   821→
   822→global variables
   823→
   824→typed constants that need storage
   825→
   826→runtime state
   827→
   828→2. Stack
   829→Used for:
   830→
   831→parameters
   832→
   833→locals
   834→
   835→temporaries
   836→
   837→return address / dynamic link / static link
   838→
   839→This is where Pascal naturally lives for ordinary code.
   840→
   841→3. Heap
   842→Used for:
   843→
   844→pointers created by new
   845→
   846→dynamic records
   847→
   848→dynamic nodes for trees/lists
   849→
   850→later, strings or managed arrays if you choose
   851→
   852→4. Code/metadata space
   853→Used for:
   854→
   855→bytecode / p-code
   856→
   857→literal pools
   858→
   859→source line tables
   860→
   861→symbol tables for debugger
   862→
   863→What should heap allocation look like?
   864→For Pascal first version: explicit allocation
   865→I recommend these VM/runtime operations:
   866→
   867→HEAP_ALLOC size -> ptr
   868→
   869→HEAP_FREE ptr
   870→
   871→HEAP_ZERO ptr, size
   872→
   873→optional HEAP_REALLOC only if another language later wants it
   874→
   875→Pascal front end then maps:
   876→
   877→new(p) → allocate size of pointed-to type, assign pointer
   878→
   879→dispose(p) → free and optionally nil the pointer
   880→
   881→maybe mark/release as a nonstandard extension later
   882→
   883→That gives you a small, understandable runtime.
   884→
   885→Should the heap be malloc/free style?
   886→Yes, for the first version.
   887→
   888→Even if the source language says new/dispose, under the hood that is basically a typed wrapper around an allocator API equivalent to malloc/free. Free Pascal’s documentation explicitly distinguishes Pascal-level New/Dispose from lower-level GetMem/FreeMem, which supports this layered interpretation. 
   889→
   890→So the layering can be:
   891→
   892→VM allocator: raw blocks
   893→
   894→Pascal runtime: typed heap ops on top
   895→
   896→That is clean.
   897→
   898→Should Pascal use GC?
   899→Not initially.
   900→
   901→Reasons:
   902→
   903→not historically typical for classic Pascal
   904→
   905→adds runtime and debugger complexity
   906→
   907→muddies semantics while you are still validating the language
   908→
   909→not needed to get real Pascal programs working
   910→
   911→You can add GC later if another language benefits from it more.
   912→
   913→Good heap strategies for your VM
   914→For your hardware and emulator, here are the realistic options.
   915→
   916→Option 1 — bump allocator only
   917→Allocate by moving a heap pointer upward.
   918→
   919→No free except “reset all”.
   920→
   921→Pros
   922→
   923→tiny
   924→
   925→perfect for bring-up
   926→
   927→very easy to debug
   928→
   929→Cons
   930→
   931→cannot support true dispose
   932→
   933→leaks until program end
   934→
   935→Good for:
   936→
   937→compiler bring-up
   938→
   939→tests
   940→
   941→short-running tools
   942→
   943→Option 2 — free-list allocator
   944→Maintain block headers and a free list.
   945→
   946→Pros
   947→
   948→simple enough to implement
   949→
   950→supports dispose
   951→
   952→realistic for Pascal
   953→
   954→Cons
   955→
   956→fragmentation
   957→
   958→coalescing logic
   959→
   960→debugger must understand headers
   961→
   962→This is the best first “real” allocator.
   963→
   964→Option 3 — segregated size classes / slab style
   965→Useful later for managed runtimes or object-heavy languages.
   966→
   967→Pros
   968→
   969→fast
   970→
   971→predictable
   972→
   973→Cons
   974→
   975→more complexity
   976→
   977→overkill initially
   978→
   979→Option 4 — tracing GC
   980→Mark-sweep, copying, or generational.
   981→
   982→Pros
   983→
   984→future-proof for Lisp-like languages
   985→
   986→fewer leaks in managed languages
   987→
   988→Cons
   989→
   990→root scanning complexity
   991→
   992→interaction with stack maps
   993→
   994→debugger complexity
   995→
   996→moving GC complicates raw pointer semantics
   997→
   998→Not for version 1.
   999→
  1000→My recommendation for your first allocator
  1001→Implement two modes:
  1002→
  1003→Bring-up mode
  1004→bump allocator
  1005→
  1006→no free
  1007→
  1008→reset heap between program runs
  1009→
  1010→Real mode
  1011→free-list allocator with headers
  1012→
  1013→block size
  1014→
  1015→free/used bit
  1016→
  1017→maybe next/prev free links
  1018→
  1019→optional coalescing on free
  1020→
  1021→This gives you:
  1022→
  1023→fastest path to first success
  1024→
  1025→smooth transition to a usable Pascal runtime
  1026→
  1027→Pascal-specific semantic model for memory
  1028→Pascal usually feels cleaner than C because most ordinary objects are not heap allocated.
  1029→
  1030→Typical pattern:
  1031→
  1032→local records/arrays: stack
  1033→
  1034→globals: static
  1035→
  1036→only pointer-based structures: heap
  1037→
  1038→That means your front end should aggressively prefer stack/static placement and use heap only when the source says so.
  1039→
  1040→Suggested mapping
  1041→Variables
  1042→local scalar/record/array → frame offset
  1043→
  1044→global scalar/record/array → global segment offset
  1045→
  1046→Parameters
  1047→by value → copied into frame or parameter area
  1048→
  1049→var parameter → address passed in
  1050→
  1051→Functions/procedures
  1052→frame contains locals and bookkeeping
  1053→
  1054→static link for nested scopes
  1055→
  1056→Pointers
  1057→24-bit pointer to heap object or global/static object
  1058→
  1059→nil = 0 is simplest, unless address 0 is valid in your architecture
  1060→
  1061→Suggested p-code machine operations for memory
  1062→I would define VM instructions along these lines.
  1063→
  1064→Addressing/storage
  1065→LDG offset — load global
  1066→
  1067→STG offset — store global
  1068→
  1069→LDL offset — load local
  1070→
  1071→STL offset — store local
  1072→
  1073→LDA_LOCAL offset — push address of local
  1074→
  1075→LDA_GLOBAL offset — push address of global
  1076→
  1077→LOAD_IND size
  1078→
  1079→STORE_IND size
  1080→
  1081→Frame/nonlocal access
  1082→FOLLOW_STATIC depth
  1083→
  1084→LDN depth, offset
  1085→
  1086→STN depth, offset
  1087→
  1088→LDA_NONLOCAL depth, offset
  1089→
  1090→Heap
  1091→ALLOC size
  1092→
  1093→FREE
  1094→
  1095→MEMZERO size
  1096→
  1097→MEMCOPY size
  1098→
  1099→Checking
  1100→CHK_BOUNDS lo, hi
  1101→
  1102→CHK_NIL
  1103→
  1104→CHK_TAG if you later add tagged objects
  1105→
  1106→TRAP code
  1107→
  1108→Aggregate helpers
  1109→INDEX elem_size, low_bound
  1110→
  1111→FIELD offset
  1112→
  1113→This instruction set can support Pascal cleanly and still be reusable for other languages.
  1114→
  1115→Should the runtime be Pascal-specific or generic?
  1116→You said other languages may follow. So keep the lower runtime generic.
  1117→
  1118→Generic VM/runtime should own
  1119→raw allocation
  1120→
  1121→raw memory movement
  1122→
  1123→stack/frame model
  1124→
  1125→trap handling
  1126→
  1127→debug hooks
  1128→
  1129→primitive I/O abstraction
  1130→
  1131→Pascal front end/runtime should own
  1132→static typing rules
  1133→
  1134→new / dispose
  1135→
  1136→var parameter semantics
  1137→
  1138→nested procedure semantics
  1139→
  1140→Pascal built-ins
  1141→
  1142→Pascal standard type semantics
  1143→
  1144→That split lets you later add languages with different memory models.
  1145→
  1146→For example:
  1147→
  1148→Pascal → explicit allocation
  1149→
  1150→Lisp → GC heap
  1151→
  1152→BASIC → dynamic strings + simple reference counting
  1153→
  1154→Forth-like language → manual dictionary arena
  1155→
  1156→Same VM, different front-end/runtime layers.
  1157→
  1158→If other languages will follow, what memory model should the VM expose?
  1159→A good neutral model is:
  1160→
  1161→VM object kinds
  1162→Not source-language-visible, just runtime categories:
  1163→
  1164→raw block
  1165→
  1166→string block
  1167→
  1168→array block
  1169→
  1170→record/object block
  1171→
  1172→closure/env block
  1173→
  1174→runtime descriptor block
  1175→
  1176→You do not need this all up front, but it is helpful to reserve the concept.
  1177→
  1178→Minimal neutral API
  1179→At the VM/runtime boundary:
  1180→
  1181→alloc raw block
  1182→
  1183→free raw block
  1184→
  1185→read/write primitive
  1186→
  1187→copy/fill
  1188→
  1189→query size
  1190→
  1191→optional type tag in header
  1192→
  1193→optional finalizer hook later
  1194→
  1195→That is enough to host both manual-memory and managed-memory languages.
  1196→
  1197→Debugger implications
  1198→If memory matters, design the allocator and frames so the debugger can introspect them.
  1199→
  1200→Heap block header suggestion
  1201→Each allocated block could have:
  1202→
  1203→struct HeapHeader {
  1204→    u24 size_words;
  1205→    u24 flags;
  1206→    u24 type_id;      // optional
  1207→    u24 prev_or_next; // depending on allocator design
  1208→}
  1209→Useful flags:
  1210→
  1211→allocated/free
  1212→
  1213→Pascal object
  1214→
  1215→array
  1216→
  1217→string
  1218→
  1219→record
  1220→
  1221→internal runtime block
  1222→
  1223→maybe “poisoned/freed”
  1224→
  1225→Then your debugger can show:
  1226→
  1227→live heap blocks
  1228→
  1229→free blocks
  1230→
  1231→leaks on exit
  1232→
  1233→block ownership/type
  1234→
  1235→double free attempts
  1236→
  1237→use-after-free if you poison blocks in debug mode
  1238→
  1239→Frame record suggestion
  1240→Each call frame should expose:
  1241→
  1242→return PC
  1243→
  1244→previous frame pointer / dynamic link
  1245→
  1246→static link
  1247→
  1248→procedure id
  1249→
  1250→locals base
  1251→
  1252→eval stack base snapshot
  1253→
  1254→That makes nested Pascal debugging much easier.

  1293→

  1300→if

  1312→Phase 4 — add debugger
  1313→Debugger should show:
  1314→
  1315→p-code instruction stream
  1316→

  1318→

  1330→This will force you to keep the VM generic rather than accidentally baking Pascal into it.
