  1332→Concrete recommendation on memory policy
  1333→If I were designing this for your 24-bit platform, I would do this:
  1334→
  1335→VM v1
  1336→separate call stack and eval stack
  1337→
  1338→globals segment
  1339→
  1340→heap with bump allocator in bring-up mode
  1341→
  1342→free-list allocator in normal mode
  1343→
  1344→no GC
  1345→
  1346→explicit traps
  1347→
  1348→pointer is 24-bit
  1349→
  1350→debug headers on every heap block in debug builds
  1351→
  1352→Pascal v1
  1353→no GC
  1354→
  1355→new / dispose
  1356→
  1357→stack for locals
  1358→
  1359→globals for statics
  1360→
  1361→heap only for pointers
  1362→
  1363→optional leak report at process exit
  1364→
  1365→runtime bounds checks enabled by default
  1366→
  1367→Later multi-language extension
  1368→Add optional alternate heap managers:
  1369→
  1370→manual heap
  1371→
  1372→reference-counted heap
  1373→
  1374→tracing GC heap
  1375→
  1376→and let each front end choose one.
  1377→
  1378→That avoids forcing Pascal into a GC design it does not need, while still leaving room for Lisp-like or scripting languages later.
  1379→
  1380→Bottom line
  1381→For your layered architecture, I would treat Pascal as a typed front end over a generic p-code VM, and I would use explicit heap management, not GC, for the first runtime.
  1382→
  1383→That means:
  1384→
  1385→stack for locals and temporaries
  1386→
  1387→globals for static storage
  1388→
  1389→heap for pointer-based dynamic objects
  1390→
  1391→new / dispose at the Pascal level
  1392→
  1393→allocator API underneath that is basically malloc/free in spirit
  1394→
  1395→GC only later, as an optional runtime mode for other languages
  1396→
  1397→That is historically aligned with Pascal, technically simpler, and a better base for a debugger and for additional languages. 
  1398→
  1399→I can turn this into a concrete vm-memory.md and pcode-opcodes.md draft next.
  1400→
  1401→
  1402→Okay, in order to write tests, I'd like to introduce a p-code-assembler. Does tha make sense?  It would be a subset of Pascal not sharing Pascal idioms, but enough to create Hello World, toggle LEDs, read/write UART.   It would have a simple calling convention, not support typing per-sed, or complex data structures.  The target ISA is integer only, so no floating point.  Does this make sense?
  1403→
  1404→Yes — that makes a lot of sense.
  1405→
  1406→In fact, for your project I think a p-code assembler is one of the best early moves, because it lets you separate three problems that otherwise get tangled together:
  1407→
  1408→VM design
  1409→
  1410→runtime / calling convention design
  1411→
  1412→Pascal front-end correctness
  1413→
  1414→If you skip the assembler, every early failure is ambiguous:
  1415→
  1416→is the VM wrong?
  1417→
  1418→is the runtime wrong?
  1419→
  1420→is the code generator wrong?
  1421→
  1422→is the parser wrong?
  1423→
  1424→is the Pascal semantic analysis wrong?
  1425→
  1426→A p-code assembler gives you a way to test the VM and runtime directly, with tiny hand-written programs.
  1427→
  1428→Restating the architecture
  1429→You are proposing a layered system like this:
  1430→
  1431→Layer A — p-code VM
  1432→Executes a small instruction set.
  1433→
  1434→Layer B — p-code assembler
  1435→Human-writable source language for tests, demos, low-level runtime bring-up.
  1436→
  1437→Layer C — runtime library / monitor bindings
  1438→UART, LED, memory, traps, maybe stack/heap helpers.
  1439→
  1440→Layer D — Pascal compiler
  1441→Compiles Pascal into p-code.
  1442→
  1443→Layer E — later front ends
  1444→Other languages also target the same p-code.
  1445→
  1446→That is a very good architecture.
  1447→
  1448→The p-code assembler becomes for your VM what assembly is for a real ISA:
  1449→
  1450→a precise test tool
  1451→
  1452→a bootstrap tool
  1453→
  1454→a debugger input language
  1455→
  1456→a way to write tiny runtime pieces before Pascal is ready
  1457→
  1458→Why this is a strong idea
  1459→1. It gives you direct VM tests
  1460→You will want tests like:
  1461→
  1462→push constants, add, print result
  1463→
  1464→branch on zero
  1465→
  1466→call/return
  1467→
  1468→access locals/globals
  1469→
  1470→follow static/dynamic links if you have them
  1471→
  1472→allocate/free heap block
  1473→
  1474→write UART
  1475→
  1476→toggle LED
  1477→
  1478→bounds trap
  1479→
  1480→nil trap
  1481→
  1482→These are much easier to express in a tiny assembly-like language than in Pascal.
  1483→
  1484→2. It lets you bootstrap the runtime before Pascal exists
  1485→Before the Pascal compiler works, you still need:
  1486→
  1487→startup
  1488→
  1489→I/O shims
  1490→
  1491→debug/trap handlers
  1492→
  1493→maybe memory helpers
  1494→
  1495→A p-code assembler lets you write these as VM-level programs or modules.
  1496→
  1497→3. It clarifies the calling convention early
  1498→You said “simple calling convention.” Good.
  1499→
  1500→The assembler forces you to define:
  1501→
  1502→what a call frame looks like
  1503→
  1504→argument passing
  1505→
  1506→return values
  1507→
  1508→local storage reservation
  1509→
  1510→stack cleanup rules
  1511→
  1512→That discipline helps both the VM and Pascal backend.
  1513→
  1514→4. It gives you reproducible debugger cases
  1515→Debugger bring-up is hard if every test comes from a buggy compiler.
  1516→
  1517→With p-asm, you can hand-write:
  1518→
  1519→a three-function call chain
  1520→
  1521→a recursive countdown
  1522→
  1523→a loop with locals
  1524→
  1525→a UART output routine
  1526→
  1527→a deliberate trap
  1528→
  1529→Then verify stepping, breakpoints, stack display, and memory inspection.
  1530→
  1531→My recommendation: make it explicitly not Pascal
  1532→I agree with your instinct that it should not share Pascal idioms.
  1533→
  1534→Do not try to make it “tiny Pascal.”
  1535→
  1536→Make it look like a machine-oriented textual form.
  1537→
  1538→That keeps the layers clean:
  1539→
  1540→Pascal is a source language
  1541→
  1542→p-asm is a target-language authoring tool
  1543→
  1544→That distinction is healthy.
  1545→
  1546→What the p-code assembler should be
  1547→Think of it as:
  1548→
  1549→untyped or lightly typed
  1550→
  1551→integer-only
  1552→
  1553→explicit control flow
  1554→
  1555→explicit loads/stores
  1556→
  1557→explicit call frames
  1558→
  1559→explicit memory operations
  1560→
  1561→labels
  1562→
  1563→directives for globals, constants, strings, imports/exports
  1564→
  1565→It should feel closer to:
  1566→
  1567→bytecode assembly
  1568→
  1569→threaded-code assembly
  1570→
  1571→simple VM macro assembly
  1572→
  1573→than to Pascal or C.
  1574→
  1575→Good scope for v1
  1576→You want enough to write:
  1577→
  1578→Hello World
  1579→
  1580→LED toggle
  1581→
  1582→UART read/write
  1583→
  1584→simple loops
  1585→
  1586→simple function calls
  1587→
  1588→stack and global tests
  1589→
  1590→That suggests a very small feature set.
  1591→
  1592→Source language features
  1593→labels
  1594→
  1595→procedures/functions
  1596→
  1597→constants
  1598→
  1599→string literals
  1600→
  1601→integer literals
  1602→
  1603→comments
  1604→
  1605→directives for globals/data
  1606→
  1607→instructions
  1608→
  1609→optional macros later
  1610→
  1611→No need initially for
  1612→type declarations
  1613→
  1614→structs
  1615→
  1616→arrays in source syntax
  1617→
  1618→records
  1619→
  1620→source-level pointers
  1621→
  1622→floating point
  1623→
  1624→polymorphism
  1625→
  1626→expression parsing beyond maybe trivial immediate syntax
  1627→
  1628→A good design principle
  1629→The assembler should expose the VM’s semantics almost 1:1.
  1630→
  1631→So if the VM has an instruction like:
  1632→
  1633→PUSH 42
  1634→
  1635→LOADG uart_status
  1636→
  1637→JZ loop
  1638→
  1639→CALL putc
  1640→
  1641→RET
  1642→
  1643→then the assembler should map directly to that.
  1644→
  1645→Avoid “convenient” high-level sugar too early.
  1646→
  1647→You want this tool mainly for:
  1648→
  1649→clarity
  1650→
  1651→testing
  1652→
  1653→determinism
  1654→
  1655→not productivity.
  1656→
  1657→One key decision: stack machine vs explicit locals model
  1658→Since this is p-code, the natural design is a stack-oriented assembler.
  1659→
  1660→Example style:
  1661→
  1662→proc main
  1663→    push 72
  1664→    call putc
  1665→    push 105
  1666→    call putc
  1667→    push 10
  1668→    call putc
  1669→    halt
  1670→endproc
  1671→But for debugging and maintainability, I recommend you also support named local slots or explicit frame offsets.
  1672→
  1673→For example:
  1674→
  1675→proc blink locals 1
  1676→loop:
  1677→    push 1
  1678→    out led
  1679→    call delay
  1680→    push 0
  1681→    out led
  1682→    call delay
  1683→    jmp loop
  1684→endproc
  1685→or, more machine-like:
  1686→
  1687→proc blink 1
  1688→loop:
  1689→    push 1
  1690→    storeg LED_PORT
  1691→    call delay
  1692→    push 0
  1693→    storeg LED_PORT
  1694→    call delay
  1695→    jmp loop
  1696→endproc
  1697→That is enough.
  1698→
  1699→Suggested minimum p-code VM model
  1700→For your custom integer-only machine, I would keep the VM itself integer-only too.
  1701→
  1702→Core VM state
  1703→pc — p-code instruction pointer
  1704→
  1705→fp — frame pointer
  1706→
  1707→sp — eval stack pointer
  1708→
  1709→gp — globals base, or implicit globals segment
  1710→
  1711→maybe hp — heap pointer if heap exists now
  1712→
  1713→Core data model
  1714→one scalar word type: signed/unsigned 24-bit integer value
  1715→
  1716→addresses are also word-sized
  1717→
  1718→byte loads/stores handled explicitly if needed for UART and strings
  1719→
  1720→That is enough for quite a while.
  1721→
  1722→Suggested instruction categories
  1723→Stack / constants
  1724→push <imm>
  1725→
  1726→dup
  1727→
  1728→drop
  1729→
  1730→swap
  1731→
  1732→over
  1733→
  1734→Arithmetic / logic
  1735→add
  1736→
  1737→sub
  1738→
  1739→mul
  1740→
  1741→div
  1742→
  1743→mod
  1744→
  1745→neg
  1746→
  1747→and
  1748→
  1749→or
  1750→
  1751→xor
  1752→
  1753→not
  1754→
  1755→Comparison
  1756→eq
  1757→
  1758→ne
  1759→
  1760→lt
  1761→
  1762→le
  1763→
  1764→gt
  1765→
  1766→ge
  1767→
  1768→These can push 0/1.
  1769→
  1770→Control flow
  1771→jmp <label>
  1772→
  1773→jz <label>
  1774→
  1775→jnz <label>
  1776→
  1777→call <proc>
  1778→
  1779→ret
  1780→
  1781→halt
  1782→
  1783→trap <code>
  1784→
  1785→Frame/local/global access
  1786→enter <nlocals>
  1787→
  1788→leave
  1789→
  1790→loadl <offset>
  1791→
  1792→storel <offset>
  1793→
  1794→loadg <symbol>
  1795→
  1796→storeg <symbol>
  1797→
  1798→addrg <symbol>
  1799→
  1800→addrl <offset>
  1801→
  1802→Indirect memory
  1803→load
  1804→
  1805→store
  1806→
  1807→loadb
  1808→
  1809→storeb
  1810→
  1811→Optional runtime
  1812→alloc <size>
  1813→
  1814→free
  1815→
  1816→sys <id> or hostcall <id>
  1817→
  1818→You may not need heap yet, but having the slot reserved is useful.
  1819→
  1820→Calling convention recommendation
  1821→Since this is for bring-up and testing, make the convention brutally simple.
  1822→
  1823→Suggested convention
  1824→arguments are pushed on the eval stack
  1825→
  1826→call foo
  1827→
  1828→callee uses enter nlocals
  1829→
  1830→arguments become addressable relative to frame pointer
  1831→
  1832→return value is pushed on the eval stack before ret
  1833→
  1834→caller removes args if needed, or callee does — pick one and stick to it
  1835→
  1836→For simplicity, I would choose:
  1837→
  1838→Better v1 convention
  1839→caller pushes args
  1840→
  1841→call foo
  1842→
  1843→callee reads args at known negative offsets from fp
  1844→
  1845→callee leaves one return value on the eval stack, or none for procedures
  1846→
  1847→callee pops its frame only
  1848→
  1849→arguments are cleaned by callee or encoded in ret n
  1850→
  1851→A nice compact form is:
  1852→
  1853→ret 0 for no args
  1854→
  1855→ret 2 meaning pop 2 args and return
  1856→
  1857→or retv 2 meaning return value + pop 2 args
  1858→
  1859→But that may complicate the VM slightly.
  1860→
  1861→A simpler alternative:
  1862→
  1863→keep call frames separate from eval stack
  1864→
  1865→copy args into frame slots on call
  1866→
  1867→That is cleaner but a bit more machinery.
  1868→
  1869→For early work, either is fine. If you expect lots of debugging, a separate frame area is easier to reason about.
  1870→
  1871→For your use case, I would recommend two abstractions for I/O
  1872→Since you want UART and LED access, decide whether p-code talks to:
  1873→
  1874→Option A — VM-level abstract devices
  1875→Example:
  1876→
  1877→sys PUTC
  1878→
  1879→sys GETC
  1880→
  1881→sys LED_WRITE
  1882→
  1883→Pros
  1884→
  1885→portable across host interpreter and real hardware
  1886→
  1887→easy to test
  1888→
  1889→easier debugger integration
  1890→
  1891→Cons
  1892→
  1893→less “metal”
  1894→
  1895→Option B — memory-mapped addresses
  1896→Example:
  1897→
  1898→push UART_DATA
  1899→
  1900→push 65
  1901→
  1902→storeb
  1903→
  1904→Pros
  1905→
  1906→closer to the hardware
  1907→
  1908→fewer abstractions
  1909→
  1910→Cons
  1911→
  1912→less portable
  1913→
  1914→awkward for hosted tests
  1915→
  1916→slightly harder to evolve
  1917→
  1918→Best answer: support both
  1919→Use memory-mapped addresses in the runtime if you want, but also provide small VM/syscall abstractions for common tasks.
  1920→
  1921→For example:
  1922→
  1923→putc implemented as runtime p-asm
  1924→
  1925→inside it, either call sys PUTC on host, or do memory-mapped UART on target
  1926→
  1927→That keeps the assembler small and the runtime portable.
  1928→
  1929→Hello World / LED / UART are perfect early test cases
  1930→These are exactly the right kinds of tests.
  1931→
  1932→Hello World tests
  1933→Validates:
  1934→
  1935→string/data section handling
  1936→
  1937→loops
  1938→
  1939→loadb/storeb
  1940→
  1941→call/return
  1942→
  1943→UART output
  1944→
  1945→LED toggle tests
  1946→Validates:
  1947→
  1948→MMIO writes
  1949→
  1950→loops
  1951→
  1952→delay call structure
  1953→
  1954→integration with emulator/FPGA
  1955→
  1956→UART read/write
  1957→Validates:
  1958→
  1959→polling loops
  1960→
  1961→conditional branching
  1962→
  1963→MMIO reads and writes
  1964→
  1965→byte operations
  1966→
  1967→Those will shake out most VM bugs quickly.
  1968→
  1969→A concrete source-language sketch
  1970→Something like this would work well:
  1971→
  1972→.const UART_DATA   0xFF0100
  1973→.const UART_STATUS 0xFF0101
  1974→.const LED_PORT    0xFF0200
  1975→
  1976→.proc putc
  1977→    ; arg0 at fp-1
  1978→wait:
  1979→    push UART_STATUS
  1980→    loadb
  1981→    push 1
  1982→    and
  1983→    jz wait
  1984→
  1985→    push UART_DATA
  1986→    loadarg 0
  1987→    storeb
  1988→    ret 1
  1989→.end
  1990→
  1991→.proc puts
  1992→loop:
  1993→    loadarg 0
  1994→    loadb
  1995→    dup
  1996→    jz done
  1997→    call putc
  1998→    loadarg 0
  1999→    push 1
  2000→    add
  2001→    storearg 0
  2002→    jmp loop
  2003→done:
  2004→    drop
  2005→    ret 1
  2006→.end
  2007→
  2008→.data msg "Hello, world!\n", 0
  2009→
  2010→.proc main
  2011→    push msg
  2012→    call puts
  2013→    halt
  2014→.end
  2015→I am not saying this exact syntax is best, only that this level of abstraction is appropriate.
  2016→
  2017→One subtle point: typed vs untyped
  2018→You said “not support typing per se.”
  2019→
  2020→That is right for the assembler source language. But the VM still needs operational widths.
  2021→
  2022→Even in an “untyped” p-code assembler, you will still need distinctions like:
  2023→
  2024→word load/store
  2025→
  2026→byte load/store
  2027→
  2028→signed vs unsigned compare, maybe
  2029→
  2030→address vs integer conventions, even if both are same-sized
  2031→
  2032→So the source language can be untyped, but the instruction set should still be explicit enough to avoid ambiguity.
  2033→
  2034→For example:
  2035→
  2036→load = word
  2037→
  2038→loadb = byte
  2039→
  2040→lt = signed compare
  2041→
  2042→ltu = unsigned compare if needed later
  2043→
  2044→This will matter for UART, chars, string traversal, and array/string runtime work.
  2045→
  2046→Should the assembler include macros?
  2047→Not initially.
  2048→
  2049→For v1, I would keep it extremely small:
  2050→
  2051→labels
  2052→
  2053→constants
  2054→
  2055→procedures
  2056→
  2057→data/string directives
  2058→
  2059→instructions
  2060→
  2061→Then after the VM is stable, add:
  2062→
  2063→.macro
  2064→
  2065→.include
  2066→
  2067→conditional assembly
  2068→
  2069→symbolic frame slot aliases
  2070→
  2071→Macros are helpful later, but they can hide VM design problems early.
  2072→
  2073→What to test first
  2074→I would define a staged test suite.
  2075→
  2076→Stage 1 — pure VM instruction tests
  2077→push/add/sub
  2078→
  2079→dup/drop/swap
  2080→
  2081→compare/branch
  2082→
  2083→simple halt
  2084→
  2085→Stage 2 — procedure tests
  2086→one call
  2087→
  2088→nested calls
  2089→
  2090→recursion
  2091→
  2092→return value
  2093→
  2094→locals
  2095→
  2096→Stage 3 — memory tests
  2097→globals
  2098→
  2099→indirect load/store
  2100→
  2101→byte load/store
  2102→
  2103→string walk
  2104→
  2105→Stage 4 — device tests
  2106→UART output
  2107→
  2108→UART polling input
  2109→
  2110→LED on/off
  2111→
  2112→blink loop
  2113→
  2114→Stage 5 — error tests
  2115→bad opcode
  2116→
  2117→stack underflow
  2118→
  2119→invalid memory access
  2120→
  2121→explicit trap
  2122→
  2123→These tests should exist before Pascal codegen starts producing real programs.
  2124→
  2125→This also helps your future debugger design
  2126→A p-code assembler pairs naturally with a debugger.
  2127→
  2128→You can support debug info at two levels:
  2129→
  2130→Level 1 — instruction/label level
  2131→current pc
  2132→
  2133→current proc
  2134→
  2135→label names
  2136→
  2137→stack contents
  2138→
  2139→frame contents
  2140→
  2141→Level 2 — source mapping
  2142→p-asm source line to p-code address
  2143→
  2144→later Pascal source line to p-code address
  2145→
  2146→That means your debugger can be useful before Pascal exists.
  2147→
  2148→This is a big win.
  2149→
  2150→One thing I would avoid
  2151→I would avoid making the p-code assembler too much like a “portable assembly language for the target ISA.”
  2152→
  2153→It should target the VM, not become a second real ISA project.
  2154→
  2155→Otherwise you risk re-creating a complicated lower-level compiler target and losing the simplicity benefit.
  2156→
  2157→So keep it:
  2158→
  2159→stack-based
  2160→
  2161→compact
  2162→
  2163→procedural
  2164→
  2165→explicit
  2166→
  2167→test-friendly
  2168→
  2169→My recommendation
  2170→Yes, introduce a p-code assembler now.
  2171→
  2172→Make it:
  2173→
  2174→machine-like, not Pascal-like
  2175→
  2176→integer-only
  2177→
  2178→stack-oriented
  2179→
  2180→simple calling convention
  2181→
  2182→labels + directives + instructions
  2183→
  2184→enough for:
  2185→
  2186→Hello World
  2187→
  2188→LED toggle
  2189→
  2190→UART I/O
  2191→
  2192→loops
  2193→
  2194→calls
  2195→
  2196→globals
  2197→
  2198→byte/word memory access
  2199→
  2200→And use it for:
  2201→
  2202→VM bring-up
  2203→
  2204→runtime bring-up
  2205→
  2206→debugger bring-up
  2207→
  2208→regression tests
  2209→
  2210→hand-authored demos
  2211→
  2212→That is a very strong foundation for Pascal and later languages.

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
     6→
     7→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
     8→
     9→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    10→```bash
    11→agentrail next
    12→```
    13→Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.
    14→
    15→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    16→```bash
    17→agentrail begin
    18→```
    19→
    20→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
    21→Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.
    22→
    23→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
    24→Commit your code changes with git.
    25→
    26→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
    27→```bash
    28→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \\
    29→  --reward 1 \\
    30→  --actions "tools and approach used"
    31→```
    32→If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
    33→If the saga is finished: add `--done`
    34→
    35→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
    36→Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
    37→Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
    38→If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.
    39→
    40→Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
    41→
    42→## Project
    43→
    44→Web UI for the tc24r compiler. Live browser demos of COR24 compilation using Rust, Yew, and WebAssembly.
    45→
    46→## Related Projects
    47→
    48→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r` -- The COR24 compiler (Rust) -- this is what we embed as WASM
    49→- `~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
    50→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c` -- Tiny Macro Lisp for COR24 (C)

    52→

    54→

    60→trunk serve    # dev server with hot reload
    61→trunk build    # production build
    62→```
    63→

     3→
     4→
     5→I want to create a Yew/Rust/WASM web app debugger for my custom p-code VM, written in COR24 assembler.  I have similar debuggers for cor24 .s files and for Forth (implemented in .s and .fth) and for Macro Lisp (implemented in C (which was impemented in Rust) and .l24 lisp). So, how do I take the p-code VM and debug it.  Specifically I'm looking for the kind of displays, of what data, which stack(s), which registers, what memory, how to single step p-code.  Start out simple (no watches, no breakpoints).  I've also implemented TSO Pipeline debuggers (w/watches and breakpoints).    Need ideas for what to build, how it will look, use-cases, and how to make it extensible so I could add watches and break-points.  For now being able to see how memory is used, possibly dis-assemble.  What do I not show?  What makes sense?
     6→
     7→You want a Yew/Rust/WASM debugger UI for a custom p-code VM whose implementation lives in COR24 assembler, and you want guidance on:
     8→
     9→what to show
    10→
    11→how it should look
    12→
    13→what data matters
    14→
    15→how to single-step p-code rather than only COR24
    16→
    17→what not to show initially
    18→
    19→how to keep the design extensible for later watches and breakpoints
    20→
    21→Given your existing debugger work for COR24 assembly, Forth, Macro Lisp, and even TSO pipeline visualizations, the right move is not to build “just another machine debugger.” Build a two-level debugger with a strong bias toward the p-code semantic level, while still allowing drill-down into the COR24 implementation when needed.
    22→
    23→1. High-level architecture
    24→Core idea
    25→Your debugger should treat the system as having three layers:
    26→
    27→UI / debugger model layer in Rust/Yew/WASM
    28→
    29→VM semantic layer for p-code execution state
    30→
    31→host implementation layer for COR24 machine state
    32→
    33→That means the debugger is primarily about:
    34→
    35→current p-code instruction
    36→
    37→p-code PC / frame / stack state
    38→
    39→VM-managed memory regions
    40→
    41→mapping from p-code step -> underlying COR24 routine(s)
    42→
    43→The key design principle is:
    44→
    45→Default to showing the p-code machine, not the COR24 machine.
    46→The COR24 state is subordinate and optionally inspectable.
    47→
    48→If you do not enforce that, the debugger turns into an assembler debugger with p-code labels painted on top, which is usually the wrong abstraction for debugging compiler/runtime/VM behavior.
    49→
    50→2. What problem the debugger is solving
    51→You are debugging at least four different classes of bugs:
    52→
    53→A. VM semantic bugs
    54→Examples:
    55→
    56→wrong opcode semantics
    57→
    58→wrong stack effect
    59→
    60→incorrect frame layout
    61→
    62→incorrect local-variable addressing
    63→
    64→incorrect call/return behavior
    65→
    66→bad heap or static data addressing
    67→
    68→wrong p-code dispatch
    69→
    70→B. p-code program bugs
    71→Examples:
    72→
    73→generated p-code is wrong
    74→
    75→compiler emits invalid operand order
    76→
    77→procedure prologue/epilogue mismatch
    78→
    79→incorrect jump targets
    80→
    81→C. VM implementation bugs in COR24
    82→Examples:
    83→
    84→dispatch loop corrupts registers
    85→
    86→VM metadata in memory is wrong
    87→
    88→stack pointer update off by one word
    89→
    90→byte/word addressing bugs
    91→
    92→D. visualization / understanding needs
    93→Examples:
    94→
    95→“where is memory going?”
    96→
    97→“what frame am I in?”
    98→
    99→“why is this return address wrong?”
   100→
   101→“why did this operand get consumed?”
   102→
   103→Because these are mixed, the debugger should let the user move between:
   104→
   105→semantic view
   106→
   107→runtime layout view
   108→
   109→low-level implementation view
   110→
   111→But the semantic view should always be first-class.
   112→
   113→3. The minimum viable debugger
   114→Start with this and no more:
   115→
   116→Main panels
   117→1. Control bar
   118→Buttons:
   119→
   120→Reset
   121→
   122→Run
   123→
   124→Pause
   125→
   126→Step p-code
   127→
   128→Step micro / host
   129→
   130→Step over call
   131→
   132→Step out
   133→
   134→Rewind one step only if you later support trace history
   135→
   136→At minimum, you need only:
   137→
   138→Reset
   139→
   140→Run
   141→
   142→Pause
   143→
   144→Step p-code
   145→
   146→Step host
   147→
   148→2. Current instruction panel
   149→Show:
   150→
   151→current p-code PC
   152→
   153→current decoded p-code instruction
   154→
   155→operands
   156→
   157→stack effect hint
   158→
   159→resolved symbolic target if applicable
   160→
   161→Example:
   162→
   163→PC: 0042
   164→Opcode: CALL 17
   165→Meaning: call procedure "fib"
   166→Stack effect: ( arg0 -- ret0 )
   167→Target paddr: 0100
   168→This should be the main focal point.
   169→
   170→3. P-code stack panel
   171→Show the VM data stack in semantic order, top clearly marked.
   172→
   173→If your p-code machine uses multiple stacks, show them separately:
   174→
   175→operand / evaluation stack
   176→
   177→call stack
   178→
   179→return stack
   180→
   181→control stack
   182→
   183→display / lexical chain stack if applicable
   184→
   185→Do not collapse these conceptually if the VM distinguishes them.
   186→
   187→4. Call frame panel
   188→Show current frame:
   189→
   190→frame pointer / base pointer
   191→
   192→current procedure name or index
   193→
   194→locals
   195→
   196→parameters
   197→
   198→return address
   199→
   200→dynamic link
   201→
   202→static link, if you use one
   203→
   204→This is one of the most useful views and should probably be richer than raw memory.
   205→
   206→5. Memory map panel
   207→Show high-level regions, not only raw hex:
   208→
   209→p-code instruction area
   210→
   211→VM globals
   212→
   213→VM stack(s)
   214→
   215→frame stack
   216→
   217→heap
   218→
   219→literal pool / constants
   220→
   221→host scratch area
   222→
   223→dispatch tables
   224→
   225→A segmented bar plus detailed table works well.
   226→
   227→6. Optional low-level COR24 panel
   228→Collapsed by default.
   229→
   230→Show:
   231→
   232→COR24 PC
   233→
   234→relevant registers
   235→
   236→SP / FP
   237→
   238→selected memory window
   239→
   240→current assembler instruction
   241→
   242→This is for VM implementation debugging, not everyday use.
   243→
   244→4. What state to represent explicitly
   245→Your debugger state model should separate semantic VM state from host machine state.
   246→
   247→VM state
   248→This should be a Rust struct something like:
   249→
   250→struct VmDebugState {
   251→    running: bool,
   252→    halted: bool,
   253→
   254→    pcode_pc: u32,
   255→    current_instr: DecodedPcodeInstr,
   256→
   257→    data_stack: Vec<Value>,
   258→    call_stack: Vec<CallFrame>,
   259→    return_stack: Vec<ReturnEntry>,
   260→
   261→    globals: Vec<MemoryCell>,
   262→    heap_summary: HeapSummary,
   263→    memory_regions: Vec<MemoryRegion>,
   264→
   265→    vm_registers: VmRegisters,
   266→    dispatch_state: DispatchState,
   267→
   268→    source_map: Option<PcodeSourceMap>,
   269→}
   270→Host state
   271→struct HostDebugState {
   272→    cor24_pc: u32,
   273→    cor24_sp: u32,
   274→    cor24_fp: u32,
   275→    regs: [u32; N],
   276→    flags: HostFlags,
   277→
   278→    current_host_instr: Option<DecodedCor24Instr>,
   279→    host_memory_windows: Vec<MemoryWindow>,
   280→}
   281→Combined state
   282→struct DebuggerState {
   283→    vm: VmDebugState,
   284→    host: HostDebugState,
   285→    stepping_mode: SteppingMode,
   286→    trace: Vec<TraceEvent>,
   287→}
   288→This separation matters because it lets you:
   289→
   290→step at different abstraction levels
   291→
   292→hide irrelevant host details
   293→
   294→later add watches and breakpoints at either level
   295→
   296→5. Registers: which ones matter?
   297→You asked specifically which registers to show.
   298→
   299→Show these at the p-code level
   300→Even if they are not physical registers, invent/debug-expose them:
   301→
   302→p-code PC
   303→
   304→current frame pointer
   305→
   306→stack top index
   307→
   308→heap pointer
   309→
   310→current procedure id
   311→
   312→dispatch opcode
   313→
   314→status / halted / fault
   315→
   316→optionally last fault reason
   317→
   318→These are much more meaningful than raw COR24 registers.
   319→
   320→Show these at the host COR24 level
   321→Only show what matters to VM execution:
   322→
   323→PC
   324→
   325→SP
   326→
   327→FP or equivalent
   328→
   329→the few registers the interpreter uses as VM state carriers
   330→
   331→flags if branch/debugging needs them
   332→
   333→Do not show every machine register prominently unless the VM actually depends on them in a meaningful/debuggable way.
   334→
   335→The rule is:
   336→
   337→If a machine register is just transient interpreter plumbing, it should not dominate the UI.
   338→
   339→6. Which stacks to show?
   340→This is one of the most important design choices.
   341→
   342→If your VM has only one stack
   343→Show:
   344→
   345→top few entries
   346→
   347→full scrollable stack
   348→
   349→type or tag if available
   350→
   351→logical index from frame base
   352→
   353→If your VM has multiple stacks
   354→Show each separately, because mixing them causes confusion.
   355→
   356→Typical candidates:
   357→
   358→Data / operand stack
   359→For expression evaluation.
   360→
   361→Call stack
   362→Procedure activations.
   363→
   364→Return stack
   365→Return addresses or continuation records.
   366→
   367→Block / control stack
   368→If needed for loops, exception-like constructs, or nested control flow.
   369→
   370→Display / lexical stack
   371→If your VM supports Pascal-like nested scopes.
   372→
   373→Best practice
   374→Have a Stack Overview panel showing all stacks with:
   375→
   376→name
   377→
   378→depth
   379→
   380→top item
   381→
   382→bounds / region
   383→
   384→Then clicking one opens detailed entries.
   385→
   386→This prevents the UI from becoming too tall while still exposing structure.
   387→
   388→7. Memory: what should the user see?
   389→You should not begin with a single giant hex dump. That is useful only after you already know the bug.
   390→
   391→Instead show memory in layers.
   392→
   393→A. Memory region map
   394→This is the first thing to show.
   395→
   396→Example regions:
   397→
   398→p-code text
   399→
   400→constants
   401→
   402→globals
   403→
   404→activation record stack
   405→
   406→operand stack
   407→
   408→heap
   409→
   410→VM metadata / tables
   411→
   412→host interpreter text/data if you want advanced mode
   413→
   414→Each region should show:
   415→
   416→start
   417→
   418→end
   419→
   420→size
   421→
   422→current usage
   423→
   424→ownership / meaning
   425→
   426→This is great for understanding how memory is used.
   427→
   428→B. Region detail view
   429→When the user selects a region, show a smarter renderer.
   430→
   431→For example:
   432→
   433→p-code text
   434→Show disassembly, not hex.
   435→
   436→stack region
   437→Show stack entries and frame boundaries.
   438→
   439→globals
   440→Show symbolic names if known.
   441→
   442→heap
   443→Show blocks, tags, sizes, links, allocation status.
   444→
   445→C. Raw memory window
   446→Useful, but secondary.
   447→
   448→Hex + ASCII + semantic annotations.
   449→Only one or two windows at first.
   450→
   451→8. Disassembly: yes, but at the right level
   452→You said “possibly dis-assemble.”
   453→Yes, absolutely, but the primary disassembly should be p-code disassembly.
   454→
   455→Show p-code disassembly
   456→A scrollable listing:
   457→
   458→address
   459→
   460→opcode
   461→
   462→operands
   463→
   464→optional labels
   465→
   466→optional symbolic comments
   467→
   468→highlight current PC
   469→
   470→maybe faded marks for recent instructions
   471→
   472→Example:
   473→
   474→0038  LDC   1
   475→0039  LDC   2
   476→0040  ADD
   477→0041  STO   local[0]
   478→0042  CALL  fib
   479→0043  JMP   0050
   480→Secondary: host disassembly
   481→This is for the COR24 interpreter implementation.
   482→
   483→Useful for:
   484→
   485→dispatch bugs
   486→
   487→entry/exit sequence bugs
   488→
   489→stack corruption inside interpreter
   490→
   491→But it should be hidden or in an “implementation” tab.
   492→
   493→9. How to single-step p-code
   494→This is the hardest architectural point.
   495→
   496→You need to decide what a single p-code step means relative to the underlying COR24 interpreter.
   497→
   498→Correct model
   499→A p-code step should mean:
   500→
   501→Execute exactly one semantic p-code instruction, even if it takes many COR24 instructions.
   502→
   503→That means your VM/interpreter needs a notion of:
   504→
   505→instruction boundary
   506→
   507→before-state
   508→
   509→after-state
   510→
   511→Best implementation options
   512→Option 1: explicit interpreter hook at dispatch boundary
   513→At the start and/or end of each p-code dispatch:
   514→
   515→capture VM state snapshot
   516→
   517→notify debugger
   518→
   519→pause after one p-code instruction if stepping mode says so
   520→
   521→This is the cleanest.
   522→
   523→Pseudo-logic:
   524→
   525→loop:
   526→  fetch opcode at pcode_pc
   527→  debugger_event(BeforePcodeStep)
   528→  execute opcode
   529→  debugger_event(AfterPcodeStep)
   530→  if step_mode == PcodeSingleStep: pause
   531→  goto loop
   532→Option 2: instrument each opcode handler
   533→Each handler reports:
   534→
   535→entered opcode X
   536→
   537→completed opcode X
   538→
   539→faulted in opcode X
   540→
   541→This is useful if some handlers internally branch or call helpers.
   542→
   543→Option 3: infer boundary from host PC ranges
   544→Least desirable.
   545→
   546→You identify that host addresses A..B implement opcode ADD, etc.
   547→This is fragile and should only be supplementary.
   548→
   549→Recommendation
   550→Use explicit semantic instrumentation in the VM, even if written in COR24 assembler.
   551→
   552→You do not want the browser debugger reconstructing p-code semantics from raw host traces.
   553→
   554→10. Step modes that make sense
   555→You asked about single stepping. Start with only a few modes.
   556→
   557→1. Step p-code
   558→Executes one semantic VM instruction.
   559→
   560→This should be the default.
   561→
   562→2. Step host
   563→Executes one COR24 instruction.
   564→
   565→Advanced mode for VM debugging.
   566→
   567→3. Step over call
   568→At p-code level:
   569→
   570→if current opcode is CALL, run until return to caller
   571→
   572→otherwise same as Step p-code
   573→
   574→Very useful early.
   575→
   576→4. Step out
   577→Run until current frame exits.
   578→
   579→Useful once call frames are visible.
   580→
   581→Do not start with step into/over/out plus breakpoints plus watches plus reverse debugging. Start with p-code step and host step.
   582→
   583→11. What the UI should look like
   584→A simple layout that will work well in Yew:
   585→
   586→Top row
   587→control bar
   588→
   589→run state
   590→
   591→current mode
   592→
   593→current procedure / p-code PC
   594→
   595→Left column
   596→p-code disassembly
   597→
   598→current instruction details
   599→
   600→Center column
   601→current frame
   602→
   603→data stack
   604→
   605→call stack
   606→
   607→Right column
   608→memory region map
   609→
   610→selected memory region detail
   611→
   612→collapsed host/COR24 panel
   613→
   614→A rough mental model:
   615→
   616→+---------------------------------------------------------------+
   617→| Controls: Reset Run Pause Step-P Step-H StepOver StepOut      |
   618→+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
   619→| P-code Disassembly   | Frame / Locals       | Memory Map       |
   620→| current line hilite  | Params / Return addr | Regions          |
   621→| nearby instructions  | Dynamic/static links | Usage bars       |
   622→+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
   623→| Current Instr Detail | Data Stack           | Region Detail    |
   624→| opcode operands      | top -> bottom        | stack/heap/glob  |
   625→| stack effect         | typed/tagged         |                  |
   626→+----------------------+----------------------+------------------+
   627→| Host / COR24 (collapsed by default)                            |
   628→+---------------------------------------------------------------+
   629→This layout favors the semantic view.
   630→
   631→12. What not to show initially
   632→This is as important as what to show.
   633→
   634→Do not show everything all at once
   635→Avoid starting with:
   636→
   637→every COR24 register always visible
   638→
   639→huge hex dumps everywhere
   640→
   641→watch panes with no watch model yet
   642→
   643→source-level data if you do not have reliable symbol maps
   644→
   645→pipeline/TSO-style timing clutter unless the VM is truly concurrent
   646→
   647→microarchitectural internals irrelevant to p-code semantics
   648→
   649→Specifically avoid these mistakes
   650→Mistake 1: host state dominates screen space
   651→Then users debug the interpreter instead of the VM program.
   652→
   653→Mistake 2: raw memory before semantic views
   654→Then users hunt bytes instead of understanding frames and stacks.
   655→
   656→Mistake 3: too many panes
   657→Yew/WASM UI can become visually busy fast.
   658→
   659→Mistake 4: pretending types exist when they do not
   660→If values are untyped words, show that honestly.
   661→If tags exist, show tags.
   662→Do not invent too much false structure.
   663→
   664→13. What makes sense to annotate
   665→Your debugger gets much better if it annotates data, even lightly.
   666→
   667→Good annotations
   668→current procedure name
   669→
   670→local slot names
   671→
   672→parameter names
   673→
   674→frame boundaries
   675→
   676→jump targets with labels
   677→
   678→constants decoded symbolically
   679→
   680→stack effect hints
   681→
   682→which memory word is “return address”
   683→
   684→which words are “saved frame pointer”, “dynamic link”, etc.
   685→
   686→Minimal metadata format
   687→You likely want a sidecar debug-symbol structure:
   688→
   689→struct PcodeDebugSymbols {
   690→    procedures: Vec<ProcedureSymbol>,
   691→    labels: Vec<LabelSymbol>,
   692→    globals: Vec<GlobalSymbol>,
   693→    local_maps: HashMap<ProcId, Vec<LocalSymbol>>,
   694→    comments: HashMap<u32, String>,
   695→}
   696→This can come from:
   697→
   698→the p-code assembler/compiler
   699→
   700→manually authored metadata
   701→
   702→generated tables
   703→
   704→This is optional at first but highly valuable.
   705→
   706→14. Use-cases the debugger should support
   707→Design around concrete scenarios.
   708→
   709→Use-case 1: basic instruction semantics
   710→“I step ADD and want to see two operands consumed and one result produced.”
   711→
   712→Needed views:
   713→
   714→current opcode
   715→
   716→operand stack before/after
   717→
   718→p-code PC change
   719→
   720→Use-case 2: call/return correctness
   721→“I call procedure P and want to confirm frame layout and return address.”
   722→
   723→Needed views:
   724→
   725→current frame
   726→
   727→call stack
   728→
   729→locals/params
   730→
   731→step over / step out
   732→
   733→Use-case 3: jump target correctness
   734→“I think a conditional branch goes to the wrong offset.”
   735→
   736→Needed views:
   737→
   738→p-code disassembly
   739→
   740→current instruction detail
   741→
   742→stack top / condition value
   743→
   744→target label
   745→
   746→Use-case 4: memory corruption
   747→“My locals get overwritten after nested calls.”
   748→
   749→Needed views:
   750→
   751→frame panel
   752→
   753→memory region map
   754→
   755→stack region detail
   756→
   757→maybe host step for interpreter bug
   758→
   759→Use-case 5: dispatch implementation bug
   760→“The p-code ADD handler works unless preceded by CALL.”
   761→
   762→Needed views:
   763→
   764→p-code step
   765→
   766→then drill down to host/COR24 step
   767→
   768→host registers / host PC / selected memory window
   769→
   770→This is why dual-level stepping matters.
   771→
   772→15. Extensibility for watches and breakpoints
   773→You asked how to make it extensible.
   774→
   775→Use an event-driven debugger core
   776→Your execution engine should emit events like:
   777→
   778→enum DebugEvent {
   779→    VmReset,
   780→    BeforePcodeStep { pc: u32, instr: DecodedPcodeInstr },
   781→    AfterPcodeStep { pc: u32, instr: DecodedPcodeInstr },
   782→    BeforeHostStep { pc: u32 },
   783→    AfterHostStep { pc: u32 },
   784→    MemoryWrite { addr: u32, old: u32, new: u32 },
   785→    StackPush { stack: StackKind, value: Value },
   786→    StackPop { stack: StackKind, value: Value },
   787→    Call { target: u32, new_frame: CallFrame },
   788→    Return { from: u32, to: u32 },
   789→    Fault { reason: VmFault },
   790→}
   791→This makes later features natural.
   792→
   793→Breakpoints later become predicates on events/state
   794→Examples:
   795→
   796→p-code PC == 42
   797→
   798→procedure == fib
   799→
   800→memory write to address range
   801→
   802→stack depth exceeds N
   803→
   804→call to procedure X
   805→
   806→Watches later become derived views
   807→Examples:
   808→
   809→show local[3]
   810→
   811→show global G
   812→
   813→show memory[0x1234]
   814→
   815→show stack top
   816→
   817→show heap usage
   818→
   819→If your debugger core already maintains explicit state snapshots and events, these are straightforward.
   820→
   821→16. Suggested data model for the UI
   822→A clean Yew architecture would separate:
   823→
   824→A. immutable-ish snapshot state
   825→What the VM looks like now.
   826→
   827→B. derived selectors
   828→Functions that compute:
   829→
   830→visible stack slice
   831→
   832→current procedure
   833→
   834→current frame locals
   835→
   836→current region occupancy
   837→
   838→surrounding disassembly window
   839→
   840→C. UI state
   841→What the user has expanded/selected:
   842→
   843→selected stack
   844→
   845→selected memory region
   846→
   847→selected disassembly line
   848→
   849→show host panel or not
   850→
   851→current stepping mode
   852→
   853→That keeps the rendering logic sane.
   854→
   855→17. A good phased plan
   856→Phase 1: semantic debugger only
   857→Build:
   858→
   859→controls
   860→
   861→current p-code instruction
   862→
   863→p-code disassembly
   864→
   865→operand stack
   866→
   867→call stack / frame view
   868→
   869→memory region map
   870→
   871→No raw host state yet except maybe a tiny status line.
   872→
   873→Phase 2: region detail
   874→Add:
   875→
   876→frame-aware stack rendering
   877→
   878→globals rendering
   879→
   880→heap summary
   881→
   882→selected memory region viewer
   883→
   884→Phase 3: host drill-down
   885→Add:
   886→
   887→COR24 PC / registers
   888→
   889→host disassembly around PC
   890→
   891→step host
   892→
   893→Phase 4: richer step control
   894→Add:
   895→
   896→step over
   897→
   898→step out
   899→
   900→run until next call/return/fault
   901→
   902→Phase 5: event history
   903→Add:
   904→
   905→last N events
   906→
   907→last memory writes
   908→
   909→last stack operations
   910→
   911→This is often more useful than watches early on.
   912→
   913→Phase 6: breakpoints and watches
   914→Only after the above feels solid.
   915→
   916→18. One especially useful feature: “why did this change?”
   917→Even without watches, you can add a very strong aid:
   918→
   919→Delta highlighting between p-code steps
   920→After each step, highlight:
   921→
   922→which stack entries changed
   923→
   924→which memory words changed
   925→
   926→which frame fields changed
   927→
   928→how p-code PC changed
   929→
   930→This can be presented as:
   931→
   932→Step 41 -> 42
   933→- data stack: pop 2, pop 3, push 5
   934→- frame unchanged
   935→- globals unchanged
   936→- pcode_pc: 0040 -> 0041
   937→This is incredibly useful and much cheaper than a full reverse debugger.
   938→
   939→19. What likely matters less at first
   940→Unless your VM really depends on them, I would postpone:
   941→
   942→cycle counts
   943→
   944→host instruction counts per p-code op
   945→
   946→pipeline timing
   947→
   948→cache-like effects
   949→
   950→raw binary sections of the host interpreter
   951→
   952→complex heap graph visualizations
   953→
   954→expression evaluators for watch windows
   955→
   956→source-level AST rendering
   957→
   958→You can add these later, but they are not the shortest path to a useful p-code debugger.
   959→
   960→20. My recommendation: the initial concrete screen
   961→If I were building v1, I would implement exactly this:
   962→
   963→Top
   964→Reset | Run | Pause | Step P | Step H | Over | Out
   965→
   966→status: Running / Paused / Faulted
   967→
   968→p-code PC, current proc, current frame depth
   969→
   970→Left pane
   971→P-code Disassembly
   972→
   973→20-50 instructions around PC
   974→
   975→current line highlighted
   976→
   977→labels shown
   978→
   979→target lines lightly marked
   980→
   981→Middle pane top
   982→Current Instruction
   983→
   984→opcode
   985→
   986→operands
   987→
   988→short semantic description
   989→
   990→stack effect
   991→
   992→Middle pane middle
   993→Operand Stack
   994→
   995→top first
   996→
   997→changed values highlighted
   998→
   999→Middle pane bottom
  1000→Call Frame
  1001→
  1002→proc name
  1003→
  1004→params
  1005→
  1006→locals
  1007→
  1008→return addr
  1009→
  1010→links
  1011→
  1012→Right pane top
  1013→Memory Regions
  1014→
  1015→segmented bar
  1016→
  1017→usage stats
  1018→
  1019→Right pane bottom
  1020→Region Detail
  1021→
  1022→selected region rendered semantically
  1023→
  1024→Bottom collapsed drawer
  1025→Host / COR24
  1026→
  1027→PC
  1028→
  1029→SP/FP
  1030→
  1031→a few regs
  1032→
  1033→nearby host disassembly
  1034→
  1035→That is enough to be genuinely useful.
  1036→
  1037→21. Short answer to “what do I not show?”
  1038→Do not lead with:
  1039→
  1040→full machine-state complexity
  1041→
  1042→giant raw hex memory dumps
  1043→
  1044→microarchitectural detail
  1045→
  1046→all registers
  1047→
  1048→watch/breakpoint UI shells before the stepping model is correct
  1049→
  1050→Do show:
  1051→
  1052→p-code PC
  1053→
  1054→decoded p-code instruction
  1055→
  1056→semantic stacks
  1057→
  1058→call frame
  1059→
  1060→memory regions
  1061→
  1062→deltas after each step
  1063→
  1064→optional low-level drill-down
  1065→
  1066→22. Design mantra
  1067→A good debugger for a VM answers these questions quickly:
  1068→
  1069→What p-code instruction am I on?
  1070→
  1071→What abstract machine state does it see?
  1072→
  1073→What changed when I stepped it?
  1074→
  1075→Where am I in procedure/frame/memory terms?
  1076→
  1077→Can I drill down to the COR24 implementation when needed?
  1078→
  1079→If your UI answers those five well, it will already be strong.
  1080→
  1081→23. Best next artifact to create
  1082→The next thing worth writing is a short design doc with sections like:
  1083→
  1084→goals
  1085→
  1086→non-goals
  1087→
  1088→abstraction layers
  1089→
  1090→debugger state model
  1091→
  1092→event model
  1093→
  1094→initial screen layout
  1095→
  1096→step semantics
  1097→
  1098→extensibility points
  1099→
  1100→After that, define the Rust structs for:
  1101→
  1102→VmDebugState
  1103→
  1104→HostDebugState
  1105→
  1106→DebugEvent
  1107→
  1108→MemoryRegion
  1109→
  1110→CallFrame
  1111→
  1112→DecodedPcodeInstr
  1113→
  1114→Then build the UI against mocked JSON snapshots before wiring it to the live VM.
  1115→
  1116→I can draft that design doc and the initial Rust/Yew type definitions next.
  1117→
  1118→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
     6→
     7→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
     8→
     9→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    10→```bash
    11→agentrail next
    12→```
    13→Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.
    14→
    15→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    16→```bash
    17→agentrail begin
    18→```
    19→
    20→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
    21→Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.
    22→
    23→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
    24→Commit your code changes with git.
    25→
    26→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
    27→```bash
    28→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
    29→  --reward 1 \
    30→  --actions "tools and approach used"
    31→```
    32→If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
    33→If the saga is finished: add `--done`
    34→
    35→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
    36→Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
    37→Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
    38→If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.
    39→
    40→Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
    41→
    42→## Project: web-tf24a -- Forth Debugger on COR24
    43→
    44→Browser-based Forth debugger running the tf24a DTC Forth interpreter on the cor24-rs emulator via WASM.
    45→
    46→### Build and Serve
    47→
    48→```bash
    49→trunk build                    # Build WASM to dist/
    50→./scripts/serve.sh             # Dev server on port 9181
    51→./scripts/build-pages.sh       # Release build to pages/ for GitHub Pages
    52→cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings  # Lint
    53→cargo fmt --all                # Format
    54→```
    55→
    56→### Utilities
    57→
    58→- `ep2ms` — returns milliseconds since epoch; use for `?ts=` cache-busting on image URLs in README (e.g. `screenshot.png?ts=$(ep2ms)`)
    59→
    60→### Architecture
    61→
    62→- **Trunk** builds the WASM binary and serves it
    63→- **cor24-emulator** provides `EmulatorCore` + `Assembler` (path dep to `../../sw-embed/cor24-rs`)
    64→- **Yew 0.21** CSR framework for the UI
    65→- Assembly files in `asm/` are `include_str!`'d and assembled at runtime
    66→- UART I/O bridges user input to the Forth interpreter running in the emulator
    67→
    68→### Key Files
    69→
    70→- `src/debugger.rs` -- Main debugger component (emulator loop, UI panels)
    71→- `src/config.rs` -- ForthTier enum (multi-tier assembly) + StackSize
    72→- `src/demos.rs` -- Demo registry (embedded .fth files)
    73→- `demos/*.fth` -- Forth demo source files
    74→- `asm/forth-bootstrap.s` -- Phase 1 tf24a Forth kernel (copied from tf24a)
    75→- `asm/forth-interpreter.s` -- Phase 4 full interpreter (copied from tf24a)
    76→- `index.html` -- Entry point with high-contrast dark theme
    77→- `src/debugger.css` -- Debugger panel styling
    78→- `build.rs` -- Build script (BUILD_SHA, BUILD_HOST, BUILD_TIMESTAMP)
    79→- `scripts/build-pages.sh` -- Release build to pages/ for GitHub Pages
    80→- `.github/workflows/pages.yml` -- Deploy pages/ on push to main
    81→
    82→### COR24 Register Allocation (from tf24a)
    83→
    84→- r0 = W (work/scratch)
    85→- r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, SRAM ~0x0F0000 growing down)
    86→- r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
    87→- sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
    88→- fp = available as extra scratch
    89→- Cell size = 3 bytes (24-bit words)
    90→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
     6→
     7→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
     8→
     9→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    10→```bash
    11→agentrail next
    12→```
    13→Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.
    14→
    15→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    16→```bash
    17→agentrail begin
    18→```
    19→
    20→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
    21→Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.
    22→
    23→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
    24→Commit your code changes with git.
    25→
    26→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
    27→```bash
    28→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \\
    29→  --reward 1 \\
    30→  --actions "tools and approach used"
    31→```
    32→If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
    33→If the saga is finished: add `--done`
    34→
    35→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
    36→Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
    37→Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
    38→If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.
    39→
    40→Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
    41→
    42→## Project
    43→
    44→Web UI for Tiny Macro Lisp on COR24. Browser-based Lisp REPL running on the COR24 emulator.
    45→
    46→## Related Projects
    47→
    48→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c` -- The Lisp implementation (C)
    49→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r` -- COR24 compiler (Rust)
    50→- `~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
    51→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-coding` -- Coding skills domain
    52→
    53→## Available Task Types
    54→
    55→`rust-project-init`, `rust-clippy-fix`, `yew-component`, `wasm-build`, `c-compile-fix`, `pre-commit`
    56→
    57→## Build
    58→
    59→Edition 2024 for any Rust code. Never suppress warnings.
    60→
    61→**Always use scripts, never run trunk directly:**
    62→
    63→```bash
    64→./scripts/serve.sh          # Dev server on port 9135
    65→./scripts/build-all.sh      # Recompile tml24c + build pages/ for GitHub Pages
    66→```
    67→
    68→`build-all.sh` does two things:
    69→1. Recompiles all 5 REPL variants (bare/minimal/standard/full/scheme) from tml24c via tc24r
    70→2. Builds WASM into `dist/` (gitignored), then rsyncs to `pages/` (preserving `.nojekyll`)
    71→
    72→**Before committing pages/ changes, always run `./scripts/build-all.sh`.**
    73→
    74→The `pages/` directory is committed and deployed via GitHub Actions (`.github/workflows/pages.yml`).
    75→Do NOT run `trunk build` or `trunk serve` directly — use the scripts.
    76→

1→
     2→
     3→
     4→
     5→How would I implement Pascal from scratch for a custom 24-bit ISA?  I have a working C compiler and Assembler (and emulator and FPGA hardware).  Was Pascal bootstapped in assembler or C and then written in Pascal?  Are there open source implementations with liberal licenses (BSD, MIT) that I can look at?  I do not want to port from a 16-bit or 32-bit system.  I prefer to write-from-scratch, clean-room implementation, possibly using already-written Pascal souce examples for testing my compiler.  I also want a debugger.  How should I start?  
     6→
     7→
     8→Thought for 36s
     9→Given your constraints, I would not start by trying to build a self-hosting Pascal compiler immediately.
    10→
    11→You already have the right base:
    12→
    13→working assembler
    14→
    15→working C compiler
    16→
    17→emulator + FPGA target
    18→
    19→desire for a clean-room implementation
    20→
    21→debugger requirement from day one
    22→
    23→That points to a practical architecture:
    24→
    25→Recommended architecture
    26→Build Pascal in three stages:
    27→
    28→Pascal front end in C
    29→
    30→lexer
    31→
    32→parser
    33→
    34→AST
    35→
    36→symbol tables
    37→
    38→type checker
    39→
    40→simple error recovery
    41→
    42→Portable intermediate form
    43→
    44→either a small stack VM / p-code
    45→
    46→or a very small typed IR of your own
    47→
    48→Back ends
    49→
    50→first: interpreter for the IR/p-code on the host
    51→
    52→second: native code generator for your 24-bit ISA
    53→
    54→later: optional self-hosting compiler in Pascal
    55→
    56→That is the lowest-risk path because it lets you validate semantics before you fight register allocation, calling conventions, and codegen bugs.
    57→
    58→Historically, how Pascal was bootstrapped
    59→The earliest Pascal compiler was not originally bootstrapped in assembler in the way many people assume. Niklaus Wirth’s first FORTRAN-based attempt was unsuccessful; the successful implementation was done in a C-like language called Scallop and then translated by hand into Pascal for bootstrapping. Many later Pascal compilers became self-hosting. A famous exception is GNU Pascal, which was written in C. Turbo Pascal, much later, was famously written in assembly for speed and size. 
    60→
    61→So the historical pattern is roughly:
    62→
    63→prototype in another language
    64→
    65→get a working compiler
    66→
    67→rewrite or re-express the compiler in Pascal
    68→
    69→use the previous compiler to compile the new compiler
    70→
    71→That is exactly the path I would recommend for your ISA as well.
    72→
    73→What to target first: native code or p-code?
    74→For your situation, I would start with a Pascal-S / Pascal-P style subset and a p-code-like intermediate machine.
    75→
    76→Why:
    77→
    78→Pascal semantics are nontrivial enough already: nested procedures, static scoping, sets, records, packed/unpacked data, file types, pass-by-reference, variant records, etc.
    79→
    80→your ISA is 24-bit, which means you will be making many nonstandard representation decisions anyway
    81→
    82→a p-code interpreter gives you a correctness oracle before native codegen
    83→
    84→Historically, Pascal-P was created specifically as a compiler porting kit: Pascal source compiled to p-code for a virtual stack machine, then interpreted on each target. That was done to make Pascal portable. 
    85→
    86→For a custom ISA, that strategy is still excellent.
    87→
    88→What subset should you implement first
    89→Do not begin with full ISO Pascal.
    90→
    91→Begin with a clean educational subset:
    92→
    93→Phase 0: expression language
    94→integers only
    95→
    96→constants
    97→
    98→variables
    99→
   100→assignment
   101→
   102→arithmetic
   103→
   104→comparisons
   105→
   106→if, while
   107→
   108→begin ... end
   109→
   110→writeln-like builtin for debugging
   111→
   112→Phase 1: real Pascal block structure
   113→program
   114→
   115→declarations
   116→
   117→scalar types: integer, boolean, char
   118→
   119→arrays with fixed bounds
   120→
   121→records
   122→
   123→procedures/functions
   124→
   125→lexical nesting
   126→
   127→local variables
   128→
   129→pass-by-value and var parameters
   130→
   131→Phase 2: features that force runtime design
   132→strings or packed char arrays
   133→
   134→sets
   135→
   136→pointers
   137→
   138→heap
   139→
   140→files
   141→
   142→case statements
   143→
   144→standard procedures/functions
   145→
   146→Phase 3: optional/full language
   147→ISO-ish cleanup
   148→
   149→conformance tests
   150→
   151→self-hosting subset
   152→
   153→For your first compiler, avoid:
   154→
   155→packed types
   156→
   157→files
   158→
   159→variant records
   160→
   161→conformant arrays
   162→
   163→procedural parameters
   164→
   165→goto
   166→
   167→real numbers
   168→
   169→Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.
   170→
   171→Clean-room strategy
   172→Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.
   173→
   174→A good clean-room posture is:
   175→
   176→read language reports and public docs
   177→
   178→study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers
   179→
   180→do not copy implementation code
   181→
   182→write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen
   183→
   184→use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs
   185→
   186→That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.
   187→
   188→Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
   189→Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:
   190→
   191→Stage A — host-side compiler in C
   192→Run on your current host machine.
   193→
   194→Outputs either:
   195→
   196→textual p-code / bytecode
   197→
   198→or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA
   199→
   200→This lets you iterate quickly.
   201→
   202→Stage B — target runtime
   203→On your ISA:
   204→
   205→tiny startup
   206→
   207→memory map
   208→
   209→stack/heap model
   210→
   211→runtime support calls
   212→
   213→optional p-code interpreter
   214→
   215→Stage C — native backend
   216→Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.
   217→
   218→Stage D — self-hosting subset
   219→Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.
   220→
   221→IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
   222→For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.
   223→
   224→Why:
   225→
   226→expressions map naturally
   227→
   228→nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early
   229→
   230→procedure calls are simpler
   231→
   232→p-code history gives you many good precedents
   233→
   234→A tiny IR might look like:
   235→
   236→LDC k load constant
   237→
   238→LD level, offset
   239→
   240→ST level, offset
   241→
   242→ADD SUB MUL DIV
   243→
   244→EQ NE LT LE GT GE
   245→
   246→JMP label
   247→
   248→JZ label
   249→
   250→CALL proc_id
   251→
   252→RET
   253→
   254→ENTER locals_size
   255→
   256→LEAVE
   257→
   258→LDI STI for indirect access
   259→
   260→IDX for array indexing
   261→
   262→FIELD offset
   263→
   264→Then later map those to your 24-bit ISA.
   265→
   266→The hardest design decisions you need early
   267→These are the places where Pascal compilers get real:
   268→
   269→1. Lexical nesting and static links
   270→Pascal’s nested procedures are one of the biggest differences from C.
   271→
   272→You need a decision for nonlocal access:
   273→
   274→static link per frame
   275→
   276→or display
   277→
   278→or closure-like environment objects
   279→
   280→For a first implementation, use static links. They are simpler and faithful to classic Pascal.
   281→
   282→Each frame should contain:
   283→
   284→return address
   285→
   286→dynamic link
   287→
   288→static link
   289→
   290→locals
   291→
   292→temporaries / spills if needed
   293→
   294→2. Data layout on a 24-bit ISA
   295→Decide up front:
   296→
   297→size of integer
   298→
   299→size of pointer
   300→
   301→alignment rules
   302→
   303→representation of boolean
   304→
   305→char width
   306→
   307→array bound representation
   308→
   309→set representation
   310→
   311→If your ISA is naturally 24-bit, I would strongly consider:
   312→
   313→integer = signed 24-bit
   314→
   315→pointer = 24-bit
   316→
   317→boolean = 24-bit 0/1
   318→
   319→char = 8-bit, but stored with clear load/store conventions
   320→
   321→align aggregates to 24-bit word boundaries unless you have compelling byte-density reasons
   322→
   323→Packed representations can wait.
   324→
   325→3. Calling convention
   326→Define this before codegen:
   327→
   328→who pushes params
   329→
   330→who cleans stack
   331→
   332→how function result is returned
   333→
   334→where static link goes
   335→
   336→which registers are caller/callee saved
   337→
   338→how large structs/records are passed
   339→
   340→For simplicity:
   341→
   342→caller pushes arguments left-to-right or right-to-left, but pick one and freeze it
   343→
   344→caller also pushes static link
   345→
   346→callee builds frame
   347→
   348→return value in a dedicated register if scalar, hidden pointer if aggregate
   349→
   350→4. Runtime model
   351→You need a tiny Pascal runtime for:
   352→
   353→startup
   354→
   355→bounds-check failure
   356→
   357→nil deref failure
   358→
   359→division by zero
   360→
   361→heap alloc/free if pointers are supported
   362→
   363→I/O stubs
   364→
   365→exit / halt
   366→
   367→Keep it tiny and callable from generated code.
   368→
   369→Debugger: how to design it from the start
   370→You said you also want a debugger. Good. Add debug support immediately, not later.
   371→
   372→I would design the compiler around a symbol-rich listing/debug model.
   373→
   374→Emit these artifacts for every compile
   375→token dump
   376→
   377→AST dump
   378→
   379→symbol table dump
   380→
   381→frame layout report
   382→
   383→IR listing
   384→
   385→native assembly listing
   386→
   387→source line map
   388→
   389→procedure metadata
   390→
   391→Runtime debugger features
   392→At minimum:
   393→
   394→single-step by source line
   395→
   396→single-step by IR instruction
   397→
   398→breakpoints by source line
   399→
   400→show call stack
   401→
   402→show current procedure
   403→
   404→show locals/parameters
   405→
   406→inspect static chain
   407→
   408→inspect arbitrary memory
   409→
   410→inspect globals
   411→
   412→inspect heap blocks
   413→
   414→show array bounds and current indices
   415→
   416→trap on runtime errors
   417→
   418→Best UI split
   419→You already have Rust/Yew/WASM skills, so I would split it this way:
   420→
   421→compiler/debug metadata generator in C
   422→
   423→emulator instrumentation hooks in Rust or current emulator code
   424→
   425→browser debugger UI in Yew/WASM
   426→
   427→Views worth having:
   428→
   429→source pane
   430→
   431→current AST / scope pane
   432→
   433→call stack pane
   434→

   436→
   437→registers pane
   438→
   439→memory pane
   440→
   441→static-chain visualization
   442→
   443→disassembly pane
   444→

   446→
   447→For Pascal specifically, one killer feature is a scope-aware variable inspector:
   448→show locals, parameters, globals, and nonlocals via static-link traversal.

   450→Recommended project plan
   451→Milestone 1 — define the language subset
   452→Write these documents first:
   453→
   454→language.md
   455→
   456→runtime.md
   457→
   458→abi.md
   459→
   460→ir.md
   461→
   462→debug-format.md
   463→
   464→test-plan.md
   465→
   466→This matters more than code at the start.
   467→
   468→Milestone 2 — host interpreter for Pascal AST
   469→Before codegen, you can interpret the AST directly for a tiny subset.
   470→
   471→Pros:
   472→
   473→fastest way to validate parser + semantics
   474→
   475→easiest way to debug language rules
   476→
   477→Cons:
   478→
   479→not reusable for target execution
   480→
   481→Still, it is often worth doing for the first 2–3 milestones.
   482→
   483→Milestone 3 — p-code generator + host p-code VM
   484→This becomes your semantic reference implementation.
   485→
   486→Milestone 4 — target p-code VM on your 24-bit ISA
   487→Now real Pascal programs run on your emulator/FPGA without needing native codegen yet.
   488→
   489→Milestone 5 — native code generator
   490→Target your 24-bit ISA directly.
   491→
   492→Milestone 6 — debugger integration
   493→Hook source lines, symbols, frames, and memory inspection into the emulator UI.
   494→
   495→Milestone 7 — self-hosting subset
   496→Rewrite enough of the compiler in Pascal to compile itself.
   497→
   498→Open-source implementations worth studying
   499→You asked specifically about liberal licenses.
   500→
   501→Good for ideas / architecture
   502→Pascal-P / Pascal-P5 is historically important and directly relevant because it is a portable p-code Pascal family. The Pascal-P5 project is presented as the fifth version of the Zurich Pascal-P compiler and emphasizes ISO 7185 compliance. 
   503→
   504→Free Pascal is real, mature, and valuable to study conceptually, but its compiler itself is not BSD/MIT. Its FAQ says applications produced with its RTL are allowed to be proprietary because the RTL uses a modified LGPL exception, but that does not make the compiler source permissively licensed. 
   505→
   506→Stanford Pascal is interesting because it still uses p-code plus an interpreter, and the repo says the compiler files are Pascal/P-Code while the interpreter is in C. I did not find an explicit permissive license on the GitHub page I checked, so I would treat it as “study carefully, license unclear until verified.” 
   507→
   508→Small permissive educational repos
   509→These are useful for architecture ideas, not for production-grade reuse:
   510→
   511→JayPascal — MIT, Pascal compiler in C#. 
   512→
   513→XDPC — MIT-0, small educational Pascal compiler with native x86 codegen. Very relevant as a “small compiler that directly emits native code” example, even though it targets DOS/x86. 
   514→
   515→Qwerasdzxc/Pascal-Compiler — Apache-2.0, modular educational compiler with interpreter and C code generator. Architecturally useful because it separates lexer/parser/symbolizer/generator/runner. 
   516→
   517→One caution: many permissively licensed Pascal repos on GitHub are student or educational compilers, not robust reference implementations. They are good for structure, testing ideas, and scope control, but not for blindly trusting semantics.
   518→
   519→What I would personally avoid
   520→For your project, I would avoid:
   521→
   522→porting Turbo Pascal ideas too literally
   523→
   524→implementing full ISO Pascal before subset stability
   525→
   526→designing codegen before you define frame layout and static-scope access
   527→
   528→adding real numbers early
   529→
   530→building an optimizing compiler first
   531→
   532→trying to self-host too soon
   533→

   536→
   537→“Compile a small Pascal subset on the host into p-code, run it in a p-code VM on the emulator, and debug it with source-line stepping.”
   538→
   539→That gives you:
   540→
   541→proof of parsing
   542→
   543→proof of semantic analysis
   544→
   545→proof of runtime model
   546→
   547→proof of debugger architecture
   548→
   549→proof of target execution
   550→
   551→before native code generation complexity arrives.
